        "text": "  2148\t        <h3>Filled shape stamping (v0.45+)</h3>\n  2149\t        <p>\n  2150\t          Before v0.45 the SVG loader only traced <strong>outlines</strong>\n  2151\t          &mdash; it sampled points along each element's perimeter and\n  2152\t          painted them in order. Stroked SVGs (line drawings, contours,\n  2153\t          calligraphic strokes) worked beautifully. Filled SVGs (logos,\n  2154\t          icons, regions with solid colors and no stroke) were\n  2155\t          effectively invisible: the loader traced the outline using\n  2156\t          the fill color, but a one-pixel-thick outline doesn't read as\n  2157\t          \"a filled red square.\"\n  2158\t        </p>\n  2159\t        <p>\n  2160\t          v0.45 changes that. <code>fillShapes: true</code> (default)\n  2161\t          adds a <strong>fill pass</strong> alongside the outline pass.\n  2162\t          For each element with a recognized fill paint, the loader:\n  2163\t        </p>\n  2164\t        <ol class=\"docs-list\">\n  2165\t          <li>Computes the element's bounding box via <code>p.getBBox()</code></li>\n  2166\t          <li>Samples a regular grid of points across the bounding box, spaced at <code>0.85 &times; brushRadius</code> in grid units (tight, slightly overlapping coverage)</li>\n  2167\t          <li>For each sample, calls <code>p.isPointInFill({x, y})</code> &mdash; the browser's exact fill-test, including holes via the fill-rule</li>\n  2168\t          <li>Keeps the inside points and adds them to the trace list with the fill paint</li>\n  2169\t        </ol>\n  2170\t        <p>\n  2171\t          The fill samples come <strong>before</strong> outline samples\n  2172\t          in the array, so when both are present the outline appears on\n  2173\t          top of the fill &mdash; the natural watercolor \"wash first,\n  2174\t          ink the edge after\" order. Filled+stroked elements get both.\n  2175\t          Pure-fill elements just get stamped. Pure-stroke elements\n  2176\t          just get outlined (unchanged from v0.44).\n  2177\t        </p>\n  2178\t        <p>\n  2179\t          <strong>Stroke and fill resolve independently.</strong> Before\n  2180\t          v0.45 the resolver collapsed both to a single paint: it walked\n  2181\t          looking for any recognized stroke OR fill and used the first\n  2182\t          hit. v0.45 splits them. An element with <code>stroke=\"blue\"\n  2183\t          fill=\"yellow\"</code> resolves to {stroke: blue, fill: yellow}\n  2184\t          &mdash; the interior stamps in yellow and the outline traces\n  2185\t          in blue. <code>fill=\"none\"</code> explicitly blocks fill\n  2186\t          inheritance up the tree; <code>stroke=\"none\"</code> blocks\n  2187\t          stroke inheritance.\n  2188\t        </p>\n  2189\t        <p>\n  2190\t          <strong>Sample density &amp; cap.</strong> Spacing of\n  2191\t          <code>0.85 &times; brushRadius</code> means roughly 1.4 stamps\n  2192\t          per brush diameter. Dense enough for solid coverage with\n  2193\t          natural watercolor variation, sparse enough to avoid\n  2194\t          oversampling. There's a 10000-sample hard cap per element to\n  2195\t          protect against pathological SVGs (huge viewBoxes + tiny\n  2196\t          brush). Hitting the cap silently skips the fill for that\n  2197\t          element and continues with the outline.\n  2198\t        </p>\n  2199\t        <p>\n  2200\t          <strong>Wetness consequence.</strong> A large filled shape\n  2201\t          fires hundreds to thousands of <code>paintAt</code> calls in\n  2202\t          its bounding box. Combined with <code>approximateColor</code>,\n  2203\t          each can become 2&ndash;3 calls per stamp (one per pigment\n  2204\t          weight). The simulation handles this fine, but if you're\n  2205\t          stamping a wide solid wash of a dark color, expect a lot of\n  2206\t          wet pigment migration during the trace. <code>paperWetness('dry')</code>\n  2207\t          before the trace gives crisper edges; <code>paperWetness('wet')</code>\n  2208\t          beforehand encourages dramatic bleeding.\n  2209\t        </p>\n  2210\t        <p>\n  2211\t          <strong>Off-switch.</strong> <code>fillShapes: false</code>\n  2212\t          falls back to the v0.44 behavior: if an element has a fill\n  2213\t          but no stroke, the outline gets traced in the fill color\n  2214\t          (which is invisible for typical filled SVGs &mdash; use this\n  2215\t          flag only if you specifically want the old behavior). Filled+\n  2216\t          stroked elements still trace just the outline in this mode.\n  2217\t        </p>\n  2218\t        <p>\n  2219\t          <strong>Instant fills (v0.46+).</strong> By default, fill\n  2220\t          stamps paint <strong>synchronously</strong> the moment\n  2221\t          <code>traceSVG()</code> is called &mdash; the entire wash\n  2222\t          appears in one frame rather than animating across the\n  2223\t          duration. This is what the brain expects from a watercolor\n  2224\t          wash: you push a wet brush onto paper and the whole footprint\n  2225\t          deposits at once. The trace animation is reserved for\n  2226\t          <em>outlines</em>, which actually benefit from sequential\n  2227\t          reveal (a pen-stroke really does follow a path).\n  2228\t        </p>\n  2229\t        <p>\n  2230\t          Without instant fills, v0.45 painted the bounding-box grid in\n  2231\t          row-major order, which the eye read as \"the brush is sweeping\n  2232\t          across the shape\" &mdash; still better than tracing the\n  2233\t          perimeter, but not what a watercolorist sees. With instant\n  2234\t          fills, you get the wash-then-ink rhythm: filled regions land\n  2235\t          immediately, outlines animate over them. Pass\n  2236\t          <code>fillInstant: false</code> to restore the v0.45\n  2237\t          row-by-row sweep behavior.\n  2238\t        </p>\n  2239\t        <p>\n  2240\t          <strong>Performance note.</strong> Instant fills can fire\n  2241\t          hundreds to thousands of <code>paintAt</code> calls in a\n  2242\t          single frame for a large SVG. The simulation handles this\n  2243\t          fine on desktop hardware; on mobile or for very dense SVGs\n  2244\t          you might see a brief stutter on the first frame. If that's\n  2245\t          a concern, <code>fillInstant: false</code> spreads the work\n  2246\t          over <code>durationMs</code> at the cost of the sweep look.\n  2247\t        </p>\n  2248\t\n  2249\t        <h3>Easing curves for drawing animation</h3>\n  2250\t        <p>\n[10148 lines total]",

      "json_block": "{\"language\": \"html\", \"code\": \"        <h3>Filled shape stamping (v0.45+)</h3>\\n        <p>\\n          Before v0.45 the SVG loader only traced <strong>outlines</strong>\\n          &mdash; it sampled points along each element's perimeter and\\n          painted them in order. Stroked SVGs (line drawings, contours,\\n          calligraphic strokes) worked beautifully. Filled SVGs (logos,\\n          icons, regions with solid colors and no stroke) were\\n          effectively invisible: the loader traced the outline using\\n          the fill color, but a one-pixel-thick outline doesn't read as\\n          \\\"a filled red square.\\\"\\n        </p>\\n        <p>\\n          v0.45 changes that. <code>fillShapes: true</code> (default)\\n          adds a <strong>fill pass</strong> alongside the outline pass.\\n          For each element with a recognized fill paint, the loader:\\n        </p>\\n        <ol class=\\\"docs-list\\\">\\n          <li>Computes the element's bounding box via <code>p.getBBox()</code></li>\\n          <li>Samples a regular grid of points across the bounding box, spaced at <code>0.85 &times; brushRadius</code> in grid units (tight, slightly overlapping coverage)</li>\\n          <li>For each sample, calls <code>p.isPointInFill({x, y})</code> &mdash; the browser's exact fill-test, including holes via the fill-rule</li>\\n          <li>Keeps the inside points and adds them to the trace list with the fill paint</li>\\n        </ol>\\n        <p>\\n          The fill samples come <strong>before</strong> outline samples\\n          in the array, so when both are present the outline appears on\\n          top of the fill &mdash; the natural watercolor \\\"wash first,\\n          ink the edge after\\\" order. Filled+stroked elements get both.\\n          Pure-fill elements just get stamped. Pure-stroke elements\\n          just get outlined (unchanged from v0.44).\\n        </p>\\n        <p>\\n          <strong>Stroke and fill resolve independently.</strong> Before\\n          v0.45 the resolver collapsed both to a single paint: it walked\\n          looking for any recognized stroke OR fill and used the first\\n          hit. v0.45 splits them. An element with <code>stroke=\\\"blue\\\"\\n          fill=\\\"yellow\\\"</code> resolves to {stroke: blue, fill: yellow}\\n          &mdash; the interior stamps in yellow and the outline traces\\n          in blue. <code>fill=\\\"none\\\"</code> explicitly blocks fill\\n          inheritance up the tree; <code>stroke=\\\"none\\\"</code> blocks\\n          stroke inheritance.\\n        </p>\\n        <p>\\n          <strong>Sample density &amp; cap.</strong> Spacing of\\n          <code>0.85 &times; brushRadius</code> means roughly 1.4 stamps\\n          per brush diameter. Dense enough for solid coverage with\\n          natural watercolor variation, sparse enough to avoid\\n          oversampling. There's a 10000-sample hard cap per element to\\n          protect against pathological SVGs (huge viewBoxes + tiny\\n          brush). Hitting the cap silently skips the fill for that\\n          element and continues with the outline.\\n        </p>\\n        <p>\\n          <strong>Wetness consequence.</strong> A large filled shape\\n          fires hundreds to thousands of <code>paintAt</code> calls in\\n          its bounding box. Combined with <code>approximateColor</code>,\\n          each can become 2&ndash;3 calls per stamp (one per pigment\\n          weight). The simulation handles this fine, but if you're\\n          stamping a wide solid wash of a dark color, expect a lot of\\n          wet pigment migration during the trace. <code>paperWetness('dry')</code>\\n          before the trace gives crisper edges; <code>paperWetness('wet')</code>\\n          beforehand encourages dramatic bleeding.\\n        </p>\\n        <p>\\n          <strong>Off-switch.</strong> <code>fillShapes: false</code>\\n          falls back to the v0.44 behavior: if an element has a fill\\n          but no stroke, the outline gets traced in the fill color\\n          (which is invisible for typical filled SVGs &mdash; use this\\n          flag only if you specifically want the old behavior). Filled+\\n          stroked elements still trace just the outline in this mode.\\n        </p>\\n        <p>\\n          <strong>Instant fills (v0.46+).</strong> By default, fill\\n          stamps paint <strong>synchronously</strong> the moment\\n          <code>traceSVG()</code> is called &mdash; the entire wash\\n          appears in one frame rather than animating across the\\n          duration. This is what the brain expects from a watercolor\\n          wash: you push a wet brush onto paper and the whole footprint\\n          deposits at once. The trace animation is reserved for\\n          <em>outlines</em>, which actually benefit from sequential\\n          reveal (a pen-stroke really does follow a path).\\n        </p>\\n        <p>\\n          Without instant fills, v0.45 painted the bounding-box grid in\\n          row-major order, which the eye read as \\\"the brush is sweeping\\n          across the shape\\\" &mdash; still better than tracing the\\n          perimeter, but not what a watercolorist sees. With instant\\n          fills, you get the wash-then-ink rhythm: filled regions land\\n          immediately, outlines animate over them. Pass\\n          <code>fillInstant: false</code> to restore the v0.45\\n          row-by-row sweep behavior.\\n        </p>\\n        <p>\\n          <strong>Performance note.</strong> Instant fills can fire\\n          hundreds to thousands of <code>paintAt</code> calls in a\\n          single frame for a large SVG. The simulation handles this\\n          fine on desktop hardware; on mobile or for very dense SVGs\\n          you might see a brief stutter on the first frame. If that's\\n          a concern, <code>fillInstant: false</code> spreads the work\\n          over <code>durationMs</code> at the cost of the sweep look.\\n        </p>\\n\\n        <h3>Easing curves for drawing animation</h3>\\n        <p>\\n\", \"filename\": \"/home/claude/watercolor.html\"}"

    "id": "toolu_01FtRMFiY8WZyVP674NLGujy",
    "name": "str_replace",
    "input": {
      "description": "Add v0.48 fallback rules subsection",
      "new_str": "        <p>\n          <strong>Stroke and fill resolve independently.</strong> Before\n          v0.45 the resolver collapsed both to a single paint: it walked\n          looking for any recognized stroke OR fill and used the first\n          hit. v0.45 splits them. An element with <code>stroke=\"blue\"\n          fill=\"yellow\"</code> resolves to {stroke: blue, fill: yellow}\n          &mdash; the interior stamps in yellow and the outline traces\n          in blue. <code>fill=\"none\"</code> explicitly blocks fill\n          inheritance up the tree; <code>stroke=\"none\"</code> blocks\n          stroke inheritance.\n        </p>\n\n        <h3>Fallback routing for unrecognized colors (v0.48+)</h3>\n        <p>\n          When a fill or stroke value doesn't resolve to a recognized\n          paint &mdash; an unrecognized hex with <code>approximateColor: false</code>,\n          a CSS color name not in the parser's table, both flags off\n          entirely &mdash; the loader has to choose what to do with the\n          active brush. The rule (v0.48+) is to respect which\n          <strong>attribute</strong> the author actually wrote, not\n          just whether it parsed:\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>Author wrote <code>fill=\"...\"</code> and it didn't resolve &rarr; the shape gets <strong>filled with the active brush</strong>. The author asked for a fill; we don't know the color, so we use yours.</li>\n          <li>Author wrote <code>stroke=\"...\"</code> and it didn't resolve &rarr; <strong>outline with the active brush</strong>. Same reasoning.</li>\n          <li>Author wrote both &rarr; both happen, each falling back as needed (recognized values keep their resolved paints; unrecognized values use the active brush).</li>\n          <li>Author wrote neither &rarr; <strong>outline with the active brush</strong>. The \"trace this SVG in my active brush\" contract from the earliest versions.</li>\n          <li>Author wrote <code>fill=\"none\"</code> or <code>stroke=\"none\"</code> &rarr; that paint is suppressed entirely. No fallback. The author explicitly said no.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <p>\n          Before v0.48, the fallback brush always went to the OUTLINE\n          slot regardless of which attribute the author wrote &mdash;\n          so a graphic with <code>fill=\"#2a2a2a\"</code> (off-black,\n          common in icon sets) got outlined in the active brush instead\n          of filled, leaving a hollow shape on the canvas. v0.48 routes\n          the fallback to the slot the author asked for, which makes\n          most icon-style SVGs work the way the eye expects them to.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Adjacent change:</strong> the SVG cascade now matches\n          how browsers actually render SVG. A child's explicit attribute\n          (recognized or not) takes precedence over a parent's. Before\n          v0.48 an unrecognized child <code>stroke=</code> would keep\n          walking up looking for a recognized parent value; now it\n          stops at the child. <code>&lt;g stroke=\"red\"&gt;&lt;rect\n          stroke=\"weird\"/&gt;&lt;/g&gt;</code> now outlines the rect\n          in the active brush (rect's stroke won, the value didn't\n          resolve, fallback applies), not in rose (which v0.47 would\n          have inherited from g).\n        </p>\n",
      "old_str": "        <p>\n          <strong>Stroke and fill resolve independently.</strong> Before\n          v0.45 the resolver collapsed both to a single paint: it walked\n          looking for any recognized stroke OR fill and used the first\n          hit. v0.45 splits them. Set <code>false</code> to disable trigger-color matching entirely. The old <code>colorMap</code> name still works as an alias. With v0.48+, unrecognized colors don't fall back to the brush <em>and lose their fill/outline role</em> &mdash; they keep their role (fill or stroke) and just use the active brush as the color. See \"Fallback routing\" below.</dd>\n          <dt><code>approximateColor</code> <em>(v0.43+)</em></dt>\n          <dd>When <code>true</code>, parseable colors that aren't exact triggers get decomposed into a mix of rose+yellow+blue and painted as such &mdash; e.g. <code>#FF8000</code> orange becomes ~35% rose + 100% yellow, <code>#808080</code> grey becomes equal parts of all three. Default <code>false</code>. Can be combined with <code>triggerColors</code>: triggers win for exact matches, approximation handles everything else. With v0.48+, colors that don't parse (HSL, unknown names, etc.) still respect their author-specified role &mdash; <code>fill=\"hsl(...)\"</code> fills with the active brush, <code>stroke=\"hsl(...)\"</code> outlines. See \\\"Fallback routing\\\" below.</dd>\\n1979:          <dd>When <code>true</code>, parseable colors that aren't exact triggers get decomposed into a mix of rose+yellow+blue and painted as such &mdash; e.g. <code>#FF8000</code> orange becomes ~35% rose + 100% yellow, <code>#808080</code> grey becomes equal parts of all three. Default <code>false</code>. Can be combined with <code>triggerColors</code>: triggers win for exact matches, approximation handles everything else. With v0.48+, colors that don't parse (HSL, unknown names, etc.) still respect their author-specified role &mdash; <code>fill=\\\"hsl(...)\\\"</code> fills with the active brush, <code>stroke=\\\"hsl(...)\\\"</code> outlines. See \\\"Approximated color mixing\\\" below for the algorithm.</dd>\\n1981:          <dd>When <code>true</code> (default), elements with a recognized fill paint get their <strong>interior stamped</strong> &mdash; a regular grid of sample points inside the path, each painted with the fill paint. Elements with a stroke also get their outline traced as before; filled+stroked shapes get both. When <code>false</code>, the v0.44 behavior is preserved: filled-only shapes have their outline traced in the fill color (which is invisible for shapes without an explicit stroke). See \\\"Filled shape stamping\\\" below.</dd>\\n1983:          <dd>When <code>true</code> (default), fill stamps paint <strong>synchronously</strong> at load time instead of animating through the trace queue. The filled region appears as a wash the moment <code>traceSVG()</code> is called, and any outlines (if present) animate separately over <code>durationMs</code>. When <code>false</code>, fills animate point-by-point through the bounding-box grid alongside outlines (the v0.45 behavior, which reads as a sweep across the shape). Has no effect on stroke-only SVGs. See \\\"Filled shape stamping\\\" below for why this matters.</dd>\\n2028:          <li>Final fallback: <code>currentPigment</code></li>\\n2065:          that way. Default <code>false</code>. Can be combined with <code>triggerColors</code>: triggers win for exact matches, approximation handles everything else. With v0.48+, colors that don't parse (HSL, unknown names, etc.) still respect their author-specified role &mdash; <code>fill=\"hsl(...)\"</code> fills with the active brush, <code>stroke=\"hsl(...)\"</code> outlines. See \"Approximated color mixing\" below for the algorithm.</dd>",

      "description": "Update resolution order to v0.45+ independent semantics",
      "new_str": "        <p>\n          <strong>Resolution model (v0.45+):</strong> stroke and fill\n          resolve <em>independently</em>. The loader walks up the DOM\n          tree separately for each property, collecting the first\n          non-<code>none</code> value it finds at each level. Each\n          property contributes its own pass to the canvas &mdash; a\n          fill paint stamps the interior, a stroke paint traces the\n          outline, and an element with both gets both.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          For each property the walk goes:\n        </p>\n        <ol class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>Element's own <code>stroke=</code> / <code>fill=</code> attribute (or inline <code>style</code>)</li>\n          <li>If absent, walk up through parent <code>&lt;g&gt;</code> groups, taking the first attribute found</li>\n          <li>Stop at the <code>&lt;svg&gt;</code> root</li>\n          <li>Resolve the found value to a paint via <code>triggerColors</code> (strict) then <code>approximateColor</code> (CMY mix)</li>\n          <li>If still unresolved but the attribute was <em>present</em> somewhere, fall back to <code>opts.pigment</code> (or <code>currentPigment</code>) in that slot &mdash; see v0.48 fallback rules below</li>\n        </ol>\n        <p>\n          An explicit <code>fill=\"none\"</code> or <code>stroke=\"none\"</code>\n          stops the walk and suppresses that paint entirely. No\n          fallback is applied for explicit <code>none</code> values\n          &mdash; the author asked for nothing.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Worked example:</strong> a single SVG drives three\n          pigments in one call:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// orchard-sketch.svg\n&lt;svg viewBox=\"0 0 100 100\"&gt;\n  &lt;path d=\"M10 80 Q50 20 90 80\"  stroke=\"#FF0000\" fill=\"none\" /&gt;\n  &lt;circle cx=\"50\" cy=\"50\" r=\"20\" stroke=\"#FFFF00\" fill=\"none\" /&gt;\n  &lt;line x1=\"0\" y1=\"100\" x2=\"100\" y2=\"100\" stroke=\"#0000FF\" /&gt;\n&lt;/svg&gt;\n\n// JS \u2014 no per-element wiring needed\nwc.traceSVG(svgText, { durationMs: 1500, easing: 'penStroke' });\n// curve traces in rose, circle in yellow, baseline in blue</pre>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Backwards compatibility:</strong> the chroma-key\n          matching is strict, so existing SVGs that use black strokes,\n          neutral fills, or arbitrary colors don't trip the trigger\n          path &mdash; they fall through to <code>opts.pigment</code>\n          via the v0.48+ fallback rules (filled elements get filled\n          with it, stroked elements outlined). For a paranoid escape\n          hatch (e.g. you have an SVG that happens to use these pure\n          primaries for unrelated reasons), pass\n          <code>triggerColors: false</code>.",

'[' shrinks the brush, ']' grows it.\n// Adjusts the active brush via wc.brushSize(), then syncs the slider +\n// the displayed pixel value so the UI stays in agreement with the lib.\n// Step size is multiplicative (10% per press) so it scales naturally\n// across the full 4..200 range \u2014 small at the low end, larger at the\n// high end. Shift+[ / Shift+] take bigger jumps (25%).\nconst sizeSlider = document.getElementById('size');\nconst sizeValue  = document.getElementById('size-value');\nfunction nudgeBrushSize(direction, big) {\n  if (!sizeSlider) return;\n  const min = +sizeSlider.min;\n  const max = +sizeSlider.max;\n  const cur = wc.brushSize();\n  const factor = big ? 0.25 : 0.10;\n  const delta = Math.max(1, Math.round(cur * factor));\n  const next = Math.max(min, Math.min(max, cur + direction * delta));\n  if (next === cur) return;\n  wc.brushSize(next);\n  sizeSlider.value = String(next);\n  if (sizeValue) sizeValue.textContent = String(next);\n}\ndocument.addEventListener('keydown', (e) => {\n  if (e.target && (e.target.tagName === 'INPUT' || e.target.tagName === 'TEXTAREA')) return;\n  if (e.metaKey || e.ctrlKey || e.altKey) return;\n  if (e.key === '[') { e.preventDefault(); nudgeBrushSize(-1, e.shiftKey); }\n  else if (e.key === ']') { e.preventDefault(); nudgeBrushSize(+1, e.shiftKey); }\n});",

Now it looks like the rest.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Brought back the bracket brush-size shortcuts.</strong> <kbd>[</kbd> shrinks, <kbd>]</kbd> grows; held <kbd>Shift</kbd> takes bigger jumps. Step size is multiplicative (10% per press, 25% with Shift) so it scales naturally across the full 4&ndash;200 range. Disabled while typing into form fields.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> <strong>\"Active cells\" in the perf overlay was stuck at 0 (0.0%).</strong> The <code>perfActiveCells()</code> function was reading <code>activeRectMinX</code> / <code>activeRectMaxX</code> / etc. &mdash; identifiers that have never existed anywhere in the lib (the real bindings are <code>activeMinX</code> / <code>activeMaxX</code> / etc., without the <code>Rect</code> infix). Active cells\n\nThis one was actually fun to find \u2014 it was a **typo that's been live since v0.27**. The `perfActiveCells()` function read variables named `activeRectMinX` / `activeRectMaxX` / `activeRectMinY` / `activeRectMaxY`. None of those names exist anywhere else in the codebase. The actual bindings (defined at line 487) are `activeMinX` / `activeMaxX` / `activeMinY` / `activeMaxY` \u2014 without the `Rect` infix.\n\nWhat made it silent for so long: the function had a defensive `typeof activeRectMinX === 'undefined'` guard that returned 0 instead of throwing. So the perf overlay quietly displayed `0 (0.0%)` for \"active cells\" forever, looking like the metric just wasn't very meaningful rather than broken.\n\nNow reading the correct bindings. Verified: a fresh canvas reports 100% (entire grid active for the initial render pass, which is correct), and as paint dries and the sim's dirty rect shrinks, the percentage should drop toward zero. You'll be able to actually use the metric to confirm the active-rect optimization is doing its job.\n\n## Where these landed in the changelog\n\nAdded a \"UI polish in the same release\" section under the v0.48 changelog entry with all three fixes flagged \u2014 `fix` tags for the dropdown styling and active-cells bug, `feature` tag for the restored bracket shortcuts.     "type": "thinking",
    "thinking": " I see \u2014 the active rect is just an axis-aligned bounding box that lets the sim skip processing cells outside it entirely, so it's really an optimization. The `activeCells` metric was measuring the area of that rect, not the actual number of cells with content. For the perf overlay, I need both pieces of information: the active rect area shows the computational cost per step, while the live cell count reveals how much of that processed area actually contains data.\n\nSo my plan is to add a function that counts cells within the rect that meet the threshold criteria, then expose both metrics separately in the perf overlay and document what each one means.\n\nI'm also thinking about the performance implications \u2014 scanning the rect at 6.7Hz with up to 540k cells and multiple array reads per cell could hit 14M reads per second at worst, but that should still be manageable. The key thing is this scan only runs when perf monitoring is enabled, so there's zero overhead for normal usage.\n\nLet me start implementing this.",

      "description": "Add perfLiveCells and expose both in perfMetrics",
      "new_str": "// v0.48 \u2014 Bug fix: this function was previously reading bindings named\n// activeRectMinX / activeRectMaxX / ... which never existed anywhere in\n// the lib (the real bindings are activeMinX / activeMaxX / etc.). The\n// `typeof === 'undefined'` guard masked the typo silently, so the perf\n// overlay's \"active cells\" readout has been stuck at 0 (0.0%) since\n// perfMetrics() was added in v0.27. Now uses the correct names and\n// reports the actual sim-active bounding box.\n//\n// Note this is the AREA of the axis-aligned bounding rectangle, not\n// the count of cells with content. See perfLiveCells() below for the\n// content-aware count. The bounding-rect metric is the perf-relevant\n// one (= number of cells simStep visits per step); the live-cells\n// metric is the human-relevant one (= cells that actually have paint).\nfunction perfActiveCells() {\n  if (typeof activeMinX === 'undefined' || activeMinX > activeMaxX) return 0;\n  return (activeMaxX - activeMinX + 1) *\n         (activeMaxY - activeMinY + 1);\n}\n\n// v0.48 \u2014 Count cells inside the active rect that actually have\n// pigment/pressure above threshold. This is the metric most users\n// expect when they read \"active cells\" \u2014 actual paint, not bounding\n// box area. Two splotches in opposite corners would report ~100% on\n// the bounding-rect metric but only a few percent on this one.\n//\n// O(rect_area), but only called from perfMetrics, which the host\n// polls at ~6.7 Hz when the perf overlay is open. Zero cost when\n// perf is off (perfMetrics isn't called).\nfunction perfLiveCells() {\n  if (activeRectIsEmpty()) return 0;\n  const g0 = g[0], g1 = g[1], g2 = g[2];\n  const thr = ACTIVE_THRESHOLD;\n  let count = 0;\n  for (let y = activeMinY; y <= activeMaxY; y++) {\n    const yo = y * GW;\n    for (let x = activeMinX; x <= activeMaxX; x++) {\n      const i = yo + x;\n      if (g0[i] > thr || g1[i] > thr || g2[i] > thr || pressure[i] > thr) {\n        count++;\n      }\n    }\n  }\n  return count;\n}",

      "new_str": "          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> <strong>\"Active cells\" in the perf overlay was stuck at 0 (0.0%).</strong> The <code>perfActiveCells()</code> function was reading <code>activeRectMinX</code> / <code>activeRectMaxX</code> / etc. &mdash; identifiers that have never existed anywhere in the lib (the real bindings are <code>activeMinX</code> / <code>activeMaxX</code> / etc., without the <code>Rect</code> infix). A <code>typeof === 'undefined'</code> guard masked the typo silently, so the readout has been stuck since the function shipped in v0.27. Now reads the correct bindings.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> <strong>...but the metric itself was also misleading.</strong> Once the typo was fixed, the readout reported the <em>area of the bounding rectangle</em> the sim iterates over per step &mdash; not the count of cells with actual pigment/pressure. Two small splotches in opposite corners would show ~60% even though &lt;1% of cells had content. The overlay now reports BOTH metrics:\n            <ul>\n              <li><strong>active cells</strong> &mdash; count of cells with pigment or pressure above threshold. What people intuitively mean by \"active\"; small for typical paintings.</li>\n              <li><strong>active rect</strong> &mdash; bounding-box area the sim iterates over per step. The perf-relevant figure; gap between it and \"active cells\" tells you how loose the bbox is (large gap = paint spread out, sim doing wasted iterations).</li>\n            </ul>\n            <code>perfMetrics()</code> exposes both as <code>activeCells</code> / <code>activeRectCells</code> with matching <code>activePct</code> / <code>activeRectPct</code>. The bbox metric was previously named <code>activeCells</code>, so the rename is a soft breaking change for anyone reading <code>activeCells</code> programmatically &mdash; check <code>activeRectCells</code> if you want the old number.\n          </li>",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "Good catch \u2014 even after fixing the typo, the metric itself was misleading.\n\n## The two definitions of \"active\"\n\nThe sim has an internal **active rectangle** \u2014 an axis-aligned bounding box around all cells with paint/pressure above threshold. This bbox is a perf optimization: each step iterates the cells inside it and skips everything outside. As paint dries, the bbox shrinks; as you paint somewhere new, it grows.\n\nThe old metric reported the **area of that bbox**, called `activeCells`. That's the perf-relevant number (number of cells the sim walks per step), but it's not what the *word* \"active cells\" suggests. If you paint two small splotches in opposite corners, the bbox stretches between them and covers ~60% of the canvas \u2014 even though &lt;1% of cells actually have content. The number looks alarming but doesn't reflect what the sim is doing.\n\n## Now there are two readouts\n\n```\nactive cells   421 (0.08%)        \u2190 cells with content above threshold\nactive rect    5,329 (0.99%)      \u2190 bbox the sim iterates over\n```\n\nAfter adding a second splotch in the opposite corner:\n\n```\nactive cells   842 (0.16%)        \u2190 grew slightly (one more splotch's worth)\nactive rect    318,329 (58.95%)   \u2190 exploded (bbox now spans both corners)\n```\n\nThe **gap between the two** is the new insight. Small gap = the bbox is tight, the sim is doing roughly the work it has to. Large gap = the sim is iterating over a loose bbox with mostly empty cells, and you could tighten the rect (it auto-shrinks every 30 frames via `shrinkActiveRect`, but lazily, so transient large gaps are normal during paint\u2192dry cycles).\n\n## Implementation cost\n\n`perfLiveCells()` does an O(rect_area) scan checking each cell against `ACTIVE_THRESHOLD` for 3 pigments + pressure. Only called from `perfMetrics()`, which the host polls at ~6.7Hz when the overlay is open. Worst case (full canvas) is ~540k cells \u00d7 6.7Hz = ~3.6M comparisons/sec \u2014 sub-millisecond on any modern CPU. Zero cost when the perf overlay isn't open (no `perfMetrics()` calls).\n\n## API surface\n\n`perfMetrics()` now returns four related fields:\n\n```js\n{\n  activeCells:     <int>,    // cells with content above threshold (NEW meaning)\n  activeRectCells: <int>,    // bbox area sim iterates over (was: activeCells)\n  activePct:       <number>, // activeCells / totalCells \u00d7 100\n  activeRectPct:   <number>, // activeRectCells / totalCells \u00d7 100\n}\n```\n\nThis is a soft breaking change: anyone reading `activeCells` programmatically gets the live-cell count now instead of the bbox area. If you want the old number, read `activeRectCells`. Hover the rows in the overlay to see tooltips explaining each.",

      "description": "Add paintImage between paintText and SVG section",
      "new_str": "// ============================================================\n// RASTER IMAGE PAINTING (v0.49)\n// ============================================================\n// paintImage(source, opts?) \u2014 sample pixels from a raster image and\n// stamp watercolor paint based on their colors. Each pixel decomposes\n// into a rose+yellow+blue mix via the same CMY algorithm as\n// approximateColor in traceSVG. Sample spacing scales with brush\n// radius (same logic as the SVG outline trace v0.47 fix), so a small\n// brush yields many tight samples and a large brush yields few wide\n// ones.\n//\n// source can be:\n//   - File / Blob (from <input type=\"file\">)\n//   - HTMLImageElement (already loaded or loading)\n//   - URL string (e.g. '/path/to/photo.jpg' or data: URL)\n//\n// Returns Promise<number> resolving to the count of stamps painted.\n// Loading is async (Image.onload waits for decode); the actual\n// painting is synchronous once the image is decoded \u2014 like fillInstant\n// for SVG, the whole image appears in one frame rather than animating.\n//\n// opts (all optional):\n//   brushSize:      display pixels \u2014 drives sample spacing. Default = active brush.\n//   strength:       stamp strength 0..1. Default = active pressure.\n//   pigment:        fallback for fully-transparent/skipped pixels (rare). Default = active.\n//   alphaThreshold: 0..1, pixels below this skip. Default 0.05.\n//   density:        multiplier on sample spacing. <1 = denser (slower, more accurate),\n//                   >1 = sparser. Default 1.\n//   flipY:          mirror the image vertically. Default false.\n//   maxStamps:      hard cap on stamps to prevent runaway sampling on huge images.\n//                   Default 20000. Step is auto-adjusted upward to fit.\n//\n// Notes on color:\n//   - White pixels (R=G=B=1) decompose to weights [0,0,0] \u2192 no paint.\n//     Photo backgrounds that are pure white naturally vanish, leaving\n//     the subject. Most photos have off-white backgrounds though, so\n//     expect a faint wash anywhere.\n//   - Dark pixels (R=G=B=0) decompose to [1,1,1] \u2192 all three pigments\n//     at max \u2192 muddy dark. Photo blacks land as deep watercolor blacks\n//     (mixed, not opaque).\n//   - Saturated colors decompose intuitively: red \u2192 rose+yellow,\n//     green \u2192 yellow+blue, etc.\n//\n// Performance: the synchronous stamp pass calls paintAt up to 3\u00d7 per\n// sample (one per non-zero pigment weight). With default settings on\n// a 1024\u00d7768 photo, that's ~12k samples \u00d7 ~2 calls = ~24k paintAt\n// calls \u2014 runs in well under a second. The maxStamps cap prevents\n// pathological cases (tiny brush + huge image) from freezing the UI.\n\nfunction _loadImageSource(source) {\n  // Already-loaded image element \u2192 reuse directly\n  if (typeof HTMLImageElement !== 'undefined' && source instanceof HTMLImageElement) {\n    if (source.complete && source.naturalWidth > 0) return Promise.resolve(source);\n    return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {\n      source.addEventListener('load', () => resolve(source), { once: true });\n      source.addEventListener('error', reject, { once: true });\n    });\n  }\n  // File or Blob \u2192 object URL + Image\n  const isBlob = (typeof Blob !== 'undefined' && source instanceof Blob);\n  const isFile = (typeof File !== 'undefined' && source instanceof File);\n  if (isBlob || isFile) {\n    const url = URL.createObjectURL(source);\n    const img = new Image();\n    return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {\n      img.addEventListener('load', () => { URL.revokeObjectURL(url); resolve(img); }, { once: true });\n      img.addEventListener('error', (e) => { URL.revokeObjectURL(url); reject(e); }, { once: true });\n      img.src = url;\n    });\n  }\n  // String \u2192 URL or data URL\n  if (typeof source === 'string') {\n    const img = new Image();\n    // crossOrigin so we can read pixels from cross-origin URLs (server\n    // must send CORS headers; data: URLs and same-origin always work).\n    if (!source.startsWith('data:')) img.crossOrigin = 'anonymous';\n    return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {\n      img.addEventListener('load', () => resolve(img), { once: true });\n      img.addEventListener('error', reject, { once: true });\n      img.src = source;\n    });\n  }\n  return Promise.reject(new Error('paintImage: unsupported source type'));\n}\n\nfunction paintImage(source, opts) {\n  opts = opts || {};\n  return _loadImageSource(source).then((img) => {\n    const iw = img.naturalWidth  || img.width;\n    const ih = img.naturalHeight || img.height;\n    if (!iw || !ih) return 0;\n\n    // Draw into off-screen canvas at native resolution and read pixels\n    const off = document.createElement('canvas');\n    off.width = iw;\n    off.height = ih;\n    const ctx = off.getContext('2d', { willReadFrequently: true });\n    ctx.drawImage(img, 0, 0);\n    let pxData;\n    try {\n      pxData = ctx.getImageData(0, 0, iw, ih).data;\n    } catch (e) {\n      // Tainted canvas (cross-origin without CORS). Can't read pixels.\n      console.warn('paintImage: cannot read pixels (tainted canvas?)', e);\n      return 0;\n    }\n\n    // Aspect-fit into the active painting area, matching the SVG loader's\n    // SVG_TRACE_FIT (70% \u2014 leaves a comfortable border so paint can\n    // bleed without clipping).\n    const fitFraction = SVG_TRACE_FIT;\n    const fitScale = Math.min((GW * fitFraction) / iw, (GH * fitFraction) / ih);\n    const drawW = iw * fitScale;\n    const drawH = ih * fitScale;\n    const offsetX = (GW - drawW) * 0.5;\n    const offsetY = (GH - drawH) * 0.5;\n\n    // Sample step in image pixels. We want stamps spaced at ~0.85 *\n    // brushRadius in GRID cells, then convert that to image-pixel\n    // spacing via fitScale (grid_cells_per_image_pixel).\n    const brushPx = opts.brushSize !== undefined ? +opts.brushSize : brushSize;\n    const brushR = (brushPx / 2) / SCALE;\n    const density = opts.density !== undefined ? +opts.density : 1;\n    const targetStepGrid = Math.max(0.5, brushR * 0.85 * density);\n    let stepPx = Math.max(1, Math.round(targetStepGrid / fitScale));\n\n    // Cap total stamps: if the image+step combo would produce too many,\n    // step up the spacing until we're under the cap. Protects against\n    // huge images \u00d7 tiny brushes locking up the UI.\n    const maxStamps = opts.maxStamps !== undefined ? +opts.maxStamps : 20000;\n    const cols0 = Math.ceil(iw / stepPx);\n    const rows0 = Math.ceil(ih / stepPx);\n    if (cols0 * rows0 > maxStamps) {\n      const ratio = Math.sqrt((cols0 * rows0) / maxStamps);\n      stepPx = Math.max(1, Math.round(stepPx * ratio));\n    }\n\n    const fallbackPigment = opts.pigment !== undefined\n      ? _resolvePigmentOption(opts.pigment)\n      : currentPigment;\n    const strength = opts.strength !== undefined ? +opts.strength : _brushPressure;\n    const alphaThresh = ((opts.alphaThreshold !== undefined ? +opts.alphaThreshold : 0.05) * 255) | 0;\n    const flipY = opts.flipY === true;\n    const flipX = opts.flipX === true;\n\n    markCanvasActive();\n    const stampState = { brushR: brushR, strength: strength, pigment: fallbackPigment };\n\n    let stamps = 0;\n    for (let py = 0; py < ih; py += stepPx) {\n      // Center the sample within its block to reduce aliasing\n      const sy = Math.min(ih - 1, py + (stepPx >> 1));\n      const dyy = flipY ? (ih - 1 - sy) : sy;\n      const rowBase = dyy * iw;\n      const gy = offsetY + sy * fitScale;\n      if (gy < 0 || gy >= GH) continue;\n      for (let px = 0; px < iw; px += stepPx) {\n        const sx = Math.min(iw - 1, px + (stepPx >> 1));\n        const dxx = flipX ? (iw - 1 - sx) : sx;\n        const i = (rowBase + dxx) * 4;\n        const a = pxData[i + 3];\n        if (a < alphaThresh) continue;\n        const r = pxData[i]     / 255;\n        const g = pxData[i + 1] / 255;\n        const b = pxData[i + 2] / 255;\n        const weights = _rgbToPigmentMix([r, g, b]);\n        // Skip pure-white pixels \u2014 all weights zero, no paint deposited\n        if (weights[0] < 0.005 && weights[1] < 0.005 && weights[2] < 0.005) continue;\n        const gx = offsetX + sx * fitScale;\n        if (gx < 0 || gx >= GW) continue;\n        // Alpha factor lets semi-transparent pixels deposit less paint\n        const alphaFactor = a / 255;\n        // Reuse the SVG paint helper for consistent multi-stamp dispatch\n        _paintSvgPoint(\n          { x: gx, y: gy, paint: { type: 'mix', weights: weights } },\n          { brushR: brushR, strength: strength * alphaFactor, pigment: fallbackPigment }\n        );\n        stamps++;\n      }\n    }\n    return stamps;\n  });\n}\n\n",

    "name": "str_replace",
    "input": {
      "description": "Add v0.49 changelog entry",
      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.49 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-49\">v0.49 &mdash; Paint a raster photo as watercolor</h2>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>New <code>paintImage(source, opts?)</code> API.</strong> Accepts a <code>File</code>, <code>Blob</code>, <code>HTMLImageElement</code>, or URL string (including <code>data:</code> URLs). Samples pixels at brush-radius spacing, decomposes each RGB into a rose+yellow+blue mix via subtractive color theory (same CMY algorithm as <code>approximateColor</code> in <code>traceSVG</code>), and stamps the corresponding pigment proportions through the existing <code>_paintSvgPoint</code> multi-stamp helper. The photo materializes as a watercolor reproduction.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>UI \"Load Image\" button</strong> in the SVG controls section, paralleling \"Load SVG.\" Accepts any browser-decodable image format (JPEG, PNG, WebP, GIF, AVIF on supporting browsers). Hides the panel on load so the wash isn't obscured.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Sample spacing scales with brush radius.</strong> A large brush (~50px) yields an impressionistic feel with broad bleeding washes; a small brush (~8px) recovers more detail at the cost of more <code>paintAt</code> calls. The same 0.85&times; brushR spacing as v0.45's SVG fill stamping.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Returns <code>Promise&lt;number&gt;</code></strong> \u2014 count of stamps actually painted. Loading is async (waits for image decode); painting is synchronous once decoded, like v0.46's <code>fillInstant</code>. The promise resolves when the entire image has been stamped.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-perf\">perf</span> <strong>Built-in stamp cap.</strong> A pathological combination (huge image &times; tiny brush) could otherwise issue millions of paintAt calls and freeze the UI. <code>opts.maxStamps</code> (default 20000) defines an upper bound; if the brush-driven step would exceed it, the step is rounded up to fit. Worst case is now bounded regardless of input.</li>\n          <li><strong>How RGB decomposes to pigment weights:</strong>\n            <ul>\n              <li>Pure white &rarr; weights [0, 0, 0] &rarr; <em>no paint</em> (white backgrounds vanish naturally, leaving the subject)</li>\n              <li>Pure red &rarr; weights [1, 1, 0] &rarr; rose + yellow at full strength (orange-red)</li>\n              <li>Pure green &rarr; weights [0, 1, 1] &rarr; yellow + blue (muted dark green)</li>\n              <li>Pure blue &rarr; weights [1, 0, 1] &rarr; rose + blue (more saturated than blue alone)</li>\n              <li>Pure black &rarr; weights [1, 1, 1] &rarr; all three at max (deep muddy watercolor black)</li>\n              <li>Greys &rarr; balanced mix of all three; brownish in the middle of the range</li>\n              <li>Semi-transparent pixels deposit less paint (strength multiplied by alpha)</li>\n            </ul>\n          </li>\n          <li><strong>Options</strong> (all optional): <code>brushSize</code>, <code>strength</code>, <code>pigment</code> (fallback for skipped pixels), <code>alphaThreshold</code> (default 0.05), <code>density</code> (spacing multiplier &mdash; &lt;1 denser, &gt;1 sparser), <code>flipX</code> / <code>flipY</code>, <code>maxStamps</code> (cap, default 20000).</li>\n          <li><strong>CORS note:</strong> loading from a cross-origin URL requires the server to send <code>Access-Control-Allow-Origin</code>. Without that, the canvas becomes tainted and <code>getImageData</code> throws &mdash; the function returns 0 and logs a warning. Same-origin URLs, <code>data:</code> URLs, and Files / Blobs from <code>&lt;input type=\"file\"&gt;</code> always work.</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.48 ===== -->",
      "old_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.48 ===== -->",

There's a hard stamp cap (default 20000,\n          configurable via <code>maxStamps</code>) so pathological\n          combinations of huge images and tiny brushes can't freeze\n          the UI.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          Loading is async (the browser has to decode the image);\n          stamping is synchronous once decoded.     "type": "text",
    "text": "**v0.49 shipped.** New \"Load Image\" button next to \"Load SVG\"; under the hood, a new `paintImage(source, opts?)` API.\n\n## How it works\n\nThe SVG `approximateColor` path (v0.43) already had the right machinery for any RGB color: decompose into rose+yellow+blue weights via subtractive color theory, then stamp each pigment proportionally. v0.45's filled-shape stamping (`isPointInFill` sampling at brush-radius spacing) was the right *sampling* pattern for areas. v0.46's `fillInstant` was the right *timing* \u2014 wash materializes in one frame.\n\nRaster photos fall right into that intersection. `paintImage`:\n1. Decodes the image (async, via `<input type=\"file\">` or any URL)\n2. Draws to an off-screen canvas at native resolution\n3. Reads pixels via `getImageData`\n4. Samples at `~0.85 \u00d7 brushRadius` spacing (same as SVG fill)\n5. For each sample, decomposes RGB into pigment weights:\n   - `rose = 1 - g`\n   - `yellow = 1 - b`\n   - `blue = 1 - r`\n6. Stamps via the same `_paintSvgPoint` multi-stamp helper as SVG mix paints\n\nEverything reuses existing code paths. The whole feature is ~150 lines of new code in the lib.\n\n## What different inputs produce\n\nVerified end-to-end with synthetic test images:\n\n| Input | Stamps | Pigments used |\n|---|---|---|\n| Pure white 100\u00d7100 | **0** | none (white = no paint) |\n| Pure red 100\u00d7100 | 2500 | rose + yellow (orange-red mix) |\n| Pure black 100\u00d7100 | 2500 | rose + yellow + blue (muddy black) |\n| Half red / half white | 1250 | rose + yellow on the red half only |\n| Orange (#FF8000) | 2500 | rose at 50% strength + yellow at 100% strength |\n| Half transparent | 1250 | only the opaque half stamps |\n\nFor orange specifically, I verified the strength scaling: with `strength: 0.8` base, rose stamps land at 0.398 (\u2248 0.8 \u00d7 0.498 weight) and yellow at 0.8 (\u00d7 1.0 weight). Blue gets zero stamps because the weight is 0. The KM compositor then renders the deposited rose+yellow ratio as the visible orange.\n\n## Brush size = stylization knob\n\nSample spacing scales with brush radius, so the brush size you have selected controls the aesthetic:\n\n- **Small brush (4-8px)** \u2192 many tight samples \u2192 recognizable detail, pointillist feel\n- **Default brush (28px)** \u2192 ~12k samples on a typical photo \u2192 recognizable subject with soft watercolor edges\n- **Large brush (80-200px)** \u2192 handful of samples \u2192 impressionistic, broad wash\n\nThere's a 20k-stamp hard cap (`opts.maxStamps`) so a tiny brush on a huge photo can't lock up the UI \u2014 the step is auto-adjusted upward if needed.\n\n## What you'll notice immediately\n\n1. **White vanishes naturally.** Pure white pixels (R=G=B=255) decompose to weights [0,0,0] = no paint. So white photo backgrounds disappear, leaving the subject floating on the paper. This matches how watercolor works \u2014 you don't paint white, you leave it. *Off-white* backgrounds still deposit a faint wash, which usually reads as atmosphere rather than a flaw.\n\n2. **Black is muddy, not opaque.** Black pixels deposit all three pigments at max, which the KM compositor renders as the dark warm brown that watercolor really produces when you over-mix complementary colors. It's accurate to how real watercolorists get \"black\" (no opaque pigment).\n\n3. **It bleeds.** Each stamp behaves like a wet brush touching paper \u2014 water spreads, pigment diffuses to neighbors, edges feather. A photo passes through the simulation rather than being faithfully reproduced. Sharp edges in the source become soft watercolor edges in the output.\n\n## Things to try\n\n```js\n// From the console \u2014 paint any image you can find a URL for\nwc.paintImage('https://example.com/photo.jpg').then(n => console.log(n, 'stamps'));\n\n// From a data URL\nwc.paintImage('data:image/png;base64,...');\n\n// Crank the strength for richer color\nwc.paintImage(file, { strength: 0.9 });\n\n// Tiny brush + dense sampling for max detail\nwc.brushSize(6);\nwc.paintImage(file, { density: 0.7 });\n\n// Quick impressionist study with a big brush\nwc.brushSize(120);\nwc.paintImage(file);\n```\n\n## CORS gotcha\n\nCross-origin URLs require the server to send `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`. Without that, the canvas becomes tainted and `getImageData` throws. The function catches it, returns 0, and logs a warning. Same-origin URLs, `data:` URLs, and `File` / `Blob` from a file input always work \u2014 which is exactly what the \"Load Image\" button uses.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

      "new_str": "function _loadImageSource(source) {\n  // v0.49 fix \u2014 reject with descriptive Error objects, not raw Event\n  // instances. Browsers don't put anything useful on the error event\n  // for Image, so the catch site got \"[object Event]\" which gave the\n  // user nothing to act on. Now we construct an Error with the source\n  // info we have (File name, MIME type, size) so the message actually\n  // points at the problem \u2014 typically HEIC / TIFF / RAW formats the\n  // browser can't decode, or a corrupted file.\n  function describeSource(src) {\n    if (typeof src === 'string') {\n      return src.length > 80 ? src.slice(0, 77) + '...' : src;\n    }\n    if (typeof Blob !== 'undefined' && src instanceof Blob) {\n      const parts = [];\n      if (src.name) parts.push(src.name);\n      if (src.type) parts.push(src.type);\n      if (typeof src.size === 'number') parts.push(src.size + ' bytes');\n      return parts.length ? parts.join(', ') : 'Blob';\n    }\n    if (typeof HTMLImageElement !== 'undefined' && src instanceof HTMLImageElement) {\n      return src.src || 'HTMLImageElement';\n    }\n    return String(src);\n  }\n\n  // Already-loaded image element \u2192 reuse directly\n  if (typeof HTMLImageElement !== 'undefined' && source instanceof HTMLImageElement) {\n    if (source.complete && source.naturalWidth > 0) return Promise.resolve(source);\n    return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {\n      source.addEventListener('load', () => resolve(source), { once: true });\n      source.addEventListener('error', () => {\n        reject(new Error('Image load failed: ' + describeSource(source)));\n      }, { once: true });\n    });\n  }\n  // File or Blob \u2192 object URL + Image\n  const isBlob = (typeof Blob !== 'undefined' && source instanceof Blob);\n  const isFile = (typeof File !== 'undefined' && source instanceof File);\n  if (isBlob || isFile) {\n    // If the MIME type is set and clearly not an image, fail fast with\n    // a helpful message instead of letting the browser fail silently.\n    if (source.type && !source.type.startsWith('image/')) {\n      return Promise.reject(new Error(\n        'Not an image file: ' + describeSource(source) +\n        ' \u2014 paintImage accepts JPEG, PNG, WebP, GIF, AVIF (and SVG for browsers that decode it as a raster).'\n      ));\n    }\n    let url;\n    try {\n      url = URL.createObjectURL(source);\n    } catch (e) {\n      return Promise.reject(new Error(\n        'Failed to create object URL for ' + describeSource(source) + ': ' + e.message\n      ));\n    }\n    const img = new Image();\n    return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {\n      img.addEventListener('load', () => {\n        URL.revokeObjectURL(url);\n        if (img.naturalWidth === 0 || img.naturalHeight === 0) {\n          reject(new Error(\n            'Image decoded with zero dimensions: ' + describeSource(source) +\n            ' \u2014 file may be corrupted, or the browser cannot decode this format ' +\n            '(HEIC / HEIF on some browsers, TIFF, RAW formats, etc.).'\n          ));\n          return;\n        }\n        resolve(img);\n      }, { once: true });\n      img.addEventListener('error', () => {\n        URL.revokeObjectURL(url);\n        reject(new Error(\n          'Image decode failed: ' + describeSource(source) +\n          ' \u2014 file may be corrupted, or the browser cannot decode this format ' +\n          '(common culprits: HEIC / HEIF on some browsers, TIFF, RAW formats).'\n        ));\n      }, { once: true });\n      img.src = url;\n    });\n  }\n  // String \u2192 URL or data URL\n  if (typeof source === 'string') {\n    const img = new Image();\n    // crossOrigin so we can read pixels from cross-origin URLs (server\n    // must send CORS headers; data: URLs and same-origin always work).\n    if (!source.startsWith('data:')) img.crossOrigin = 'anonymous';\n    return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {\n      img.addEventListener('load', () => {\n        if (img.naturalWidth === 0 || img.naturalHeight === 0) {\n          reject(new Error('Image decoded with zero dimensions: ' + describeSource(source)));\n          return;\n        }\n        resolve(img);\n      }, { once: true });\n      img.addEventListener('error', () => {\n        // Distinguish CORS failures from network/decode failures by\n        // probing fetch \u2014 fetch surfaces the real reason where Image\n        // collapses everything into a generic error event.\n        let hint = '';\n        if (!source.startsWith('data:') && source.startsWith('http')) {\n          hint = ' (possible causes: network failure, 404, or cross-origin without CORS headers)';\n        }\n        reject(new Error('Image load failed: ' + describeSource(source) + hint));\n      }, { once: true });\n      img.src = source;\n    });\n  }\n  return Promise.reject(new Error(\n    'paintImage: unsupported source type ' + Object.prototype.toString.call(source) +\n    ' \u2014 expected File, Blob, HTMLImageElement, or URL string'\n  ));\n}",
      "old_str": "function _loadImageSource(source) {\n  // Already-loaded image element \u2192 reuse directly\n  if (typeof HTMLImageElement !== 'undefined' && source instanceof HTMLImageElement) {\n    if (source.complete && source.naturalWidth > 0) return Promise.resolve(source);\n    return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {\n      source.addEventListener('load', () => resolve(source), { once: true });\n      source.addEventListener('error', reject, { once: true });\n    });\n  }\n  // File or Blob \u2192 object URL + Image\n  const isBlob = (typeof Blob !== 'undefined' && source instanceof Blob);\n  const isFile = (typeof File !== 'undefined' && source instanceof File);\n  if (isBlob || isFile) {\n    const url = URL.createObjectURL(source);\n    const img = new Image();\n    return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {\n      img.addEventListener('load', () => { URL.revokeObjectURL(url); resolve(img); }, { once: true });\n      img.addEventListener('error', (e) => { URL.revokeObjectURL(url); reject(e); }, { once: true });\n      img.src = url;\n    });\n  }\n  // String \u2192 URL or data URL\n  if (typeof source === 'string') {\n    const img = new Image();\n    // crossOrigin so we can read pixels from cross-origin URLs (server\n    // must send CORS headers; data: URLs and same-origin always work).\n    if (!source.startsWith('data:')) img.crossOrigin = 'anonymous';\n    return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {\n      img.addEventListener('load', () => resolve(img), { once: true });\n      img.addEventListener('error', reject, { once: true });\n      img.src = source;\n    });\n  }\n  return Promise.reject(new Error('paintImage: unsupported source type'));\n}",

We catch that and report it instead of silently doing nothing.\n3. **Descriptive error event** \u2014 when decode actually fails, the message includes the file name, MIME, size, and the most likely culprits.\n\nThe wiring side also extracts `err.message` instead of stringifying the whole object, and it pops the panel back open so you can retry without hitting the +/\u2212 toggle.\n\n## Now try the same file again\n\nYou'll see the actual reason.       "new_str": "// v0.48 \u2014 Count cells inside the active rect that actually have\n// pigment/pressure above threshold. This is the metric most users\n// expect when they read \"active cells\" \u2014 actual paint, not bounding\n// box area. Two splotches in opposite corners would report ~100% on\n// the bounding-rect metric but only a few percent on this one.\n//\n// O(rect_area), but only called from perfMetrics, which the host\n// polls at ~6.7 Hz when the perf overlay is open. Zero cost when\n// perf is off (perfMetrics isn't called).\nfunction perfLiveCells() {\n  if (activeRectIsEmpty()) return 0;\n  const g0 = g[0], g1 = g[1], g2 = g[2];\n  const thr = ACTIVE_THRESHOLD;\n  let count = 0;\n  for (let y = activeMinY; y <= activeMaxY; y++) {\n    const yo = y * GW;\n    for (let x = activeMinX; x <= activeMaxX; x++) {\n      const i = yo + x;\n      if (g0[i] > thr || g1[i] > thr || g2[i] > thr || pressure[i] > thr) {\n        count++;\n      }\n    }\n  }\n  return count;\n}\n\n// v0.49 \u2014 Sum total pigment \"mass\" across the ENTIRE grid, not just\n// the active rect. This is the cell-level sum of suspended (g) and\n// deposited (d) pigment over all three pigment channels \u2014 the raw\n// quantity of paint on the canvas regardless of whether the cell is\n// currently \"active\" by the simulation's threshold.\n//\n// Useful for:\n//   - Verifying paint conservation (paintAt deposits add up, lift /\n//     fade subtract; with no input the total should stay flat \u00b1 noise).\n//   - Diagnosing the active-rect optimization: if pigmentTotal is\n//     non-zero but the rect is empty (or much smaller than expected),\n//     it indicates residue or cells the shrink-scan missed.\n//   - Building \"how much paint did the user apply\" UIs.\n//\n// O(N) per call (~540k cells on default grid), all 6 arrays touched\n// linearly. Cheap on modern CPUs \u2014 well under a millisecond \u2014 and\n// only called from perfMetrics (which only runs while the perf\n// overlay is open). Returns three numbers in a single object:\n//   suspended \u2014 total mass in the water layer (g[0..2])\n//   deposited \u2014 total mass fixed on paper (d[0..2])\n//   total     \u2014 suspended + deposited\nfunction perfTotalPigment() {\n  const g0 = g[0], g1 = g[1], g2 = g[2];\n  const d0 = d[0], d1 = d[1], d2 = d[2];\n  let suspended = 0, deposited = 0;\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n    suspended += g0[i] + g1[i] + g2[i];\n    deposited += d0[i] + d1[i] + d2[i];\n  }\n  return { suspended: suspended, deposited: deposited, total: suspended + deposited };\n}",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "Shipped. New row at the bottom of the perf overlay:\n\n```\npigment \u03a3    69.6 (susp 0.0 + dep 69.6)\n```\n\n## What's behind the number\n\nThe simulation stores pigment in two parallel sets of 3 Float32Arrays (one per pigment \u2014 rose, yellow, blue):\n\n- `g[0..2]` \u2014 **suspended** pigment (still moving with the water layer)\n- `d[0..2]` \u2014 **deposited** pigment (fixed on the paper, doesn't move)\n\n`perfTotalPigment()` walks the whole grid linearly (~540k cells on default) and sums all six arrays. The returned object splits the result into `suspended`, `deposited`, and `total` so you can see at a glance whether your paint is still flowing or has settled.\n\n## Why this is more honest than the cell-count metrics\n\nWalking through the test sequence makes the difference visible:\n\n| State | active cells | active rect | pigment \u03a3 |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| Fresh canvas | 0 (0.00%) | 540k (100%) | 0.00 |\n| 1 small rose splotch | 185 (0.03%) | 540k (100%) | 16.78 (all suspended) |\n| + 1 blue splotch in opposite corner | 606 (0.11%) | 540k (100%) | 69.59 (all suspended) |\n| After `dry()` | **0 (0.00%)** | **0 (0.00%)** | **69.59 (all deposited)** |\n\nThe `dry()` row is the punchline. Both cell metrics drop to zero \u2014 the sim has nothing left to *simulate* (no flow, no pressure) \u2014 but **the paint is still on the paper**. 69.59 units of pigment, now in the deposited layer. The previous metrics couldn't see this; the new metric does.\n\nThe active rect (100% in the fresh-canvas + just-painted rows) is the bounding box the sim *would* iterate if not for the dirty-rect shrink. It starts wide because the canvas was just initialized.\n\n## Diagnostic uses\n\nA few things this number lets you verify:\n\n- **Paint conservation.** Apply paint with no other inputs and the total should monotonically increase. Stop painting and let everything dry; the total should stay roughly flat (small floating-point drift from diffusion is expected). If it grows or shrinks without input, there's a leak or sink somewhere in the sim phases.\n- **Suspended \u2192 deposited transition.** During a paint stroke, you should see `suspended` rise. As cells dry, mass migrates from `suspended` to `deposited`, with the total roughly conserved.\n- **`lift` and `fade` work as expected.** Both should decrease the total. The water brush moves mass from `deposited` back into `suspended` (rewet); fade reduces both.\n- **Shrink-scan residue.** If `pigmentTotal > 0` but `activeRectCells == 0`, the shrink-scan has tightened past some cells that still hold sub-threshold residue. Usually harmless (the values are below `ACTIVE_THRESHOLD = 0.001` per channel) but if you care about exact conservation it's diagnostic.\n\n## API\n\n`perfMetrics()` now returns three additional fields:\n\n```js\n{\n  pigmentSuspended: <number>,  // sum of g[0]+g[1]+g[2] over all N cells\n  pigmentDeposited: <number>,  // sum of d[0]+d[1]+d[2] over all N cells\n  pigmentTotal:     <number>,  // pigmentSuspended + pigmentDeposited\n}\n```\n\nSame shape as the cell metrics \u2014 three flat numbers, easy to feed into a custom overlay if you're building one. Sub-millisecond cost per call (only when perf is enabled).",
    "citations": []
  }
]

    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-12T07:46:04.516627Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-12T07:48:30.994005Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "thinking",
    "thinking": " I'm looking at the current fade mechanism\u2014exponential decay with a hard cutoff at 0.001\u2014and the user wants to switch to a spring function that pulls the pigment toward zero instead. I'm considering a critically-damped spring model that would give a smooth deceleration without oscillation.\n\nThe main challenge is that a spring system needs to track both position and velocity for each cell, which means adding three new Float32Arrays (one per channel) totaling about 6.5 MB. The aesthetic difference is meaningful though: exponential decay lingers asymptotically forever, while a critically-damped spring actually reaches zero with a more natural \"settling\" feel\u2014the pigment fades faster in the middle phase rather than dragging on indefinitely.\n\nThe math works out to a displacement curve of (1 + kt)\u00b7e^(-kt), where the system starts at position 1 with zero velocity and smoothly decelerates to rest. To fade from full intensity to effectively zero takes a time proportional to 1/k, which is more predictable than exponential decay's infinite tail.\n\nI'm weighing implementation approaches: storing velocity arrays alongside position and updating them via Euler integration... ro: True spring physics\n   - Con: 3 new Float32Arrays (6.5 MB), more complex update\n   \n2. **Closed-form per cell** \u2014 track each cell's `paint_age` separately, evaluate (1+kt)e^(-kt) given age. \n   - Pro: No accumulating error\n   - Con: 3 new Float32Arrays for age, complex to handle when fresh paint is added on top of existing paint (mixed ages)\n   \n3. **Global time + per-cell start value** \u2014 capture the value at fade start, evaluate curve from there\n   - Same issue with mixed ages\n\n4. **Approximate the spring curve via piecewise function** \u2014 match exponential decay at high values while accelerating at lower values, with no extra storage but losing true spring physics\n\nFor option 1, I could integrate the spring ODE directly in place using velocity updates, though that requires storing velocity across the three pigment channels\u2014about 6.5 MB extra. Since fade is optional and storage matters, I'm wondering if there's a way to avoid that overhead entirely, maybe through a half-life-based approach instead.\n\nActually, rereading the request: they want a fade with spring-like *behavior*, not necessarily literal spring physics. The key difference is that exponential decay drops fast then plateaus, whereas a spring-like fade would stay high initially, accelerate downward, then settle\u2014almost like an ease-in-out curve. I could potentially achieve this without per-cell velocity if I track age or use an approximation, though mixed ages when new paint layers on old paint complicates things. Approach: **piecewise fade**.\n- For high values (above threshold), apply slow decay (e.g. fadeFactor close to 1)\n- For mid-range values, apply faster decay\n- For low values, accelerate the drop to 0\n\nThis gives a sigmoid-like curve without needing velocity storage. It's not a \"true\" spring but visually similar \u2014 slow start, fast middle, smooth completion.\n\nWait, let me reconsider what they're asking for. \"Spring function to pigment = 0\" could mean a few different things: actual spring physics with velocity and oscillation, a spring-like easing curve with that characteristic shape, or just exponential decay proportional to displacement. The third one is what we already have, so they probably want either true spring physics or a different curve shape altogether.\n\nGiven they asked for an \"improved fading function\" and specifically mentioned it should be \"like a spring,\" they're likely after that distinctive spring impulse response \u2014 the curve that starts slow, accelerates through the middle, and lands smoothly at zero. The cleanest approach would be to add per-cell velocity tracking.\n\nImplementing this means creating three new Float32Arrays to store velocity components, which adds about 6.5 MB of memory. The computational cost roughly doubles per fade tick \u2014 we'd be doing 3 multiplies and 3 adds plus load/store operations for each velocity and displacement value. Still very manageable performance-wise.\n\nSetting up velocity arrays and integrating a spring ODE... I'm implementing a semi-implicit Euler integrator for stability, where I update velocity first using the acceleration, then position using the new velocity. The acceleration follows the critically damped spring equation: a = -k\u00b7x - 2\u221ak\u00b7v, which ensures smooth decay without oscillation.\n\nImplementing the integration loop...\n\nThere's a subtle issue though: when new paint gets deposited into a cell, the velocity carries over from whatever state it was in before, which could be negative if it's already fading. That creates a jarring discontinuity where fresh pigment immediately starts disappearing. I should reset the velocity to zero whenever paint is added, so the spring effect restarts cleanly from rest. But I need to be careful\u2014this would interrupt any existing spring motion in pigment that's already there. The cleanest approach is probably to reset velocity only when paint is freshly deposited into a cell.\n\nImplementing the spring decay...\n\nAfter each timestep, velocity becomes negative and pulls the pigment value toward zero, but I need to clamp it at zero since pigment can't go negative. When paint is added mid-simulation, that's a discontinuous jump in the value, so I need to handle how velocity should respond to that sudden increase.\n\nThe simplest approach is resetting velocity to zero whenever paint is added\u2014the spring restarts fresh each time. This has a nice side effect: rapid painting prevents fading entirely since velocity keeps resetting, while inactive painting lets it fade naturally. The tricky part is that paint gets modified in multiple places throughout the code, so I'd need to intercept all of them, which feels fragile. An alternative would be to leave velocity alone but cap it at zero so it never becomes positive.\n\nActually, thinking about the user experience: when someone paints over a fading area, they expect the new paint to look vivid again, not continue fading. So resetting velocity in the paintAt function makes sense\u2014it's just one spot to modify, and it gives the right behavior without being too invasive. Though I should double-check how paintAt actually works, since it usually adds to the gradient array rather than directly modifying the paint values.\n\nThe real fix might be to reset velocity when paint actually gets deposited during the dry step, not during the initial paint. Let me look for where the deposited values increase in the code.\n\nActually, I'm overcomplicating this. Instead of managing velocity discontinuities, I could use a state machine approach where each cell tracks whether it's idle, holding steady, or actively decaying. That way I can control exactly when the fade begins and avoid the velocity jump altogether.\n\nBut that requires per-cell phase counters, which adds memory overhead. Let me try a different angle: track when each cell was last painted, then compute the fade as a closed-form function of elapsed time. The problem is I'd need to know the initial value at the start of the fade to apply the spring equation, and that varies per cell.\n\nStoring that initial value alongside the timestamp would mean adding even more arrays than the velocity approach, so velocity-based damping is the better choice. I'll go with that: create velocity arrays for each color channel, allocate them lazily when fade is first enabled, update both position and velocity each step using semi-implicit Euler, and zero out the velocity arrays when fade gets re-enabled after being disabled.\n\nNow I'm testing what happens when someone paints over a cell that's already been fading\u2014if the cell has accumulated downward velocity and suddenly gets more pigment added, the velocity doesn't reset, so the new paint gets pulled down faster initially. Let me trace through the math: after 5 seconds of fading with velocity at -0.3 and displacement at 0.1, adding 0.4 brings displacement to 0.5, but the velocity stays at -0.3. The next step recalculates velocity based on the spring and damping forces, which quickly brings things back into equilibrium.\n\nThe real issue is that the user sees the newly painted pigment drop away noticeably faster than fresh paint normally would, which feels wrong. I think the cleanest fix is to reset velocity to zero whenever paint gets added, but that means I need to hook into the paint functions rather than trying to detect increments inside the fade step itself. the inter-tick frames, d[] increased, that's a paint event.\n\nFade ticks every 6 frames, so between ticks paintAt runs many times. I need to compare against the d[] value from the previous fade tick. I could store an extra snapshot array to track the post-fade values, then compare at the start of the next tick to detect increases.\n\nSetting up snapshot storage... Resetting velocity on deposit...\n\nActually, I need to think about where the transition from suspended to deposited happens\u2014that's during the dry pass. And paintAt mostly modifies the suspended state anyway, not the deposited one, so the fade logic needs to target the right moment. Let me trace through the code to find where that transition occurs. I'm also noticing the existing exponential fade already has a hard cutoff at 0.001 where it snaps to zero, which creates a similar discontinuity but seems to work visually.\n\nFor the spring approach, I should apply the same strategy: detect when velocity crosses zero and the value drops below a threshold, then snap to zero. My plan is to lazily allocate the velocity arrays when fade is enabled, integrate the spring ODE in fadeStep for each cell, and snap to zero when conditions are met. I'll hold off on resetting paint increments initially and see how it looks. But wait\u2014I just realized the current exponential fade is multiplicative, so when the user paints over a faded cell, the combined value fades at the same rate without any discontinuity because of how the math works out.\n\nThe spring behaves differently. It has a fixed natural frequency regardless of the starting value, so both small and large values reach zero in the same wall-clock time, just with different velocities. With a 4-second half-life, exponential decay takes about 36 seconds to hit the floor, while a spring with the same half-life reaches near-zero in roughly 15-16 seconds and then snaps. That's much cleaner\u2014about half the time overall.\n\nNow I'm wondering if the spring's half-life parameter should mean the same thing as the exponential's. If a user sets 4 seconds, I want the value to drop to 50% at that point. Using the damped oscillator formula, I can solve for the decay constant: at 50%, kt \u2248 1.6783, so k = 0.42 for a 4-second half-life. This keeps the slider intuitive\u2014the half-life time actually corresponds to 50% remaining. After that, the spring drops faster than exponential, reaching about 15% by 8 seconds and continuing to accelerate downward.\n\nNow I'm working through the implementation details for the spring ODE. I need to set up the critical damping equation properly\u2014the standard mass-spring-damper form with the right relationship between natural frequency and decay constant. Now I need to find the specific decay constant that matches the desired half-life by solving for when the response drops to 0.5, which gives me u \u2248 1.6783, and then I can derive \u03c9\u2080 from the target half-life in seconds. I'm setting up the code to compute the damping coefficient and squared frequency from this relationship.\n\nFor the numerical integration, I'm using semi-implicit Euler which is stable for small timesteps, but I need to be careful when the decay is very fast\u2014shorter half-lives mean larger omega values that could destabilize the integration. I can either use adaptive substepping based on how large omega gets, or switch to a closed-form solution since this is a linear ODE and I can compute the exact state at any time.\n\nLet me work through the closed-form solution for critical damping. The general form is x(t) = (A + Bt)\u00b7e^(-\u03c9t), and using the initial conditions x\u2080 and v\u2080, I can solve for the constants: A = x\u2080 and B = v\u2080 + \u03c9\u00b7x\u2080.\n\nNow I'm simplifying the velocity expression by substituting these constants and expanding the algebra to get the final form.\n\nI'm implementing this as an exact numerical integration step that's unconditionally stable regardless of timestep size, though the exponential function call is computationally expensive. With hundreds of thousands of cells updating at high frequency, that's millions of exponential evaluations per second. But since omega and dt are fixed parameters, I can precompute the exponential term once rather than recalculating it for every cell.\n\nSetting up precomputed constants... Writing the fade step loop...\n\nActually, I need to verify that newX can't go negative due to overshoot. For critical damping with positive initial position and zero velocity, the solution is monotonically decreasing, so no overshoot there. But if velocity is negative from prior fading, a small position could theoretically overshoot \u2014 though the math suggests it shouldn't for this setup. I'll add a clamp just to be safe. ):\nx\u2080 = current value (post-paint), v\u2080 = old velocity (negative)\nx(t) = (x\u2080 + (v\u2080 + \u03c9x\u2080)\u00b7t)\u00b7e^(-\u03c9t)\nThe factor (x\u2080 + B\u00b7t) could go negative if B = v\u2080 + \u03c9\u00b7x\u2080 < 0, i.e., if v\u2080 < -\u03c9\u00b7x\u2080. Then x(t) crosses 0 at t = x\u2080/(-B).\n\nSo if v\u2080 is sufficiently negative, x can go negative.
