When you combine that with a painted value interrupting the decay, you could easily end up with a velocity negative enough to cause overshoot past zero.\n\nI could hook into the paint event directly to reset velocity, or I could detect new paint inside the fade step by checking if the value increased since the last tick. I should check the library to see how paintAt works.\n\nActually, I'm reconsidering whether per-cell velocity is even necessary. I could instead track the time since each cell was last painted and use a time-based decay formula where the value follows an exponential decay curve scaled by how long it's been since the paint event.\n\nThe key insight is that cells get painted at different times, so each one needs to remember when it was last painted and what value it had then. If a cell gets painted again, that timestamp and value update. This requires storing both the paint time and paint value per cell\u2014two Float32Arrays per channel, which is the same memory footprint as storing position and velocity. The paint-event formulation feels cleaner semantically though, since paint events explicitly trigger the fade reset.\n\nSetting up the data structures...\n\nIn the fade step, I compute how much time has passed since each cell was last painted, apply an exponential decay function based on that age, and clamp values below a threshold to zero.       "description": "Replace exponential fade with critically-damped spring",
      "new_str": "// PIGMENT FADE \u2014 gradual lifting of deposited pigment over time\n// ============================================================\n// v0.50 \u2014 Critically-damped spring decay toward zero (replaces\n// pure exponential).\n//\n// What changed and why:\n//   The previous implementation was pure multiplicative decay:\n//     d[k][i] *= fadeFactor    (fadeFactor = 0.5^(dt/halflife))\n//   Mathematically clean, but visually it asymptotes \u2014 the paint\n//   *never* really lets go, just gets fainter forever. A hard floor\n//   at FADE_SKIP_BELOW snapped the last sliver to 0, giving a small\n//   visible jump as the very last bit popped off.\n//\n//   The spring is a critically-damped 2nd-order system pulling each\n//   cell toward x=0 from its current pigment value. The impulse\n//   response for a cell starting at rest (v=0, x=x\u2080) is:\n//     x(t) = x\u2080 \u00b7 (1 + \u03c9\u00b7t) \u00b7 e^(-\u03c9\u00b7t)\n//   which is monotonically decreasing, smoothly approaches 0, and\n//   *reaches* 0 in finite practical time. Compared to exponential\n//   with the same half-life: same midpoint behavior, but the spring\n//   accelerates through the second half and lands cleanly instead\n//   of asymptoting. Visually: paint lingers, then decisively fades\n//   to clean paper.\n//\n//   Half-life semantics preserved. fadeHalfLifeMs still means \"time\n//   for a freshly-deposited cell to drop to 50% of its value.\" The\n//   slider keeps working identically.\n//\n// Implementation:\n//   - Per-cell velocity arrays dVel[0..2] (lazy-allocated on first\n//     fade enable, ~6.5 MB on default grid)\n//   - Closed-form one-step integration via precomputed coefficients\n//     (exp() called once at slider-change, not per cell)\n//   - Velocity is reset to 0 in the evaporation pass whenever\n//     suspended pigment settles into d[] \u2014 fresh paint always\n//     starts the spring from rest\n//   - When x falls below FADE_SKIP_BELOW, snap x and v to 0\n//\n// Suspended pigment (g[]) is intentionally NOT faded \u2014 wet strokes\n// stay vibrant until they settle into d[], then begin contributing\n// to the spring decay.\nconst FADE_TICK_INTERVAL = 6;        // 10 Hz at 60 fps; trade CPU for smoothness\nconst FADE_DEFAULT_MS = 4000;        // 4 s default half-life\nconst FADE_MIN_MS = 500;             // slider min\nconst FADE_MAX_MS = 30000;           // slider max\n// Don't run fade when there's essentially nothing left to fade \u2014\n// avoids waking render() forever to multiply already-zero values.\nconst FADE_SKIP_BELOW = 0.001;       // avg pigment per cell\n\n// Critical-damping half-life constant: for x(t) = (1+\u03c9t)\u00b7e^(-\u03c9t),\n// solving x(t)=0.5 gives \u03c9\u00b7t \u2248 1.6783. So \u03c9 = 1.6783 / T_half.\nconst FADE_SPRING_HALFLIFE_K = 1.6783;\n\nlet fadeEnabled = false;             // toggled by \"Fade painting\" button\n// v0.19 \u2014 when true (default), fade ticks trigger a full-canvas re-render\n// so cells outside the active rect stay in sync with their faded d[]\n// values. Without this, a rectangular outline appears around freshly-\n// painted strokes after a fade tick (the active rect's boundary becomes\n// visible because cells just inside the rect render at their current\n// faded value while cells just outside still show pre-fade pixels).\n// Exposed on the API as Watercolor.fadeFullRender(bool).\nlet fadeFullRender = true;\nlet fadeHalfLifeMs = FADE_DEFAULT_MS;\n\n// Spring coefficients \u2014 recomputed by setFadeHalfLifeMs whenever the\n// slider moves. All three are functions of half-life and the tick dt;\n// computing them once and reusing across all N cells per tick is what\n// makes the integration cheap.\nlet _fadeSpringDt        = FADE_TICK_INTERVAL / 60;                  // seconds per tick\nlet _fadeSpringOmega     = FADE_SPRING_HALFLIFE_K / (FADE_DEFAULT_MS / 1000);\nlet _fadeSpringOmegaDt   = _fadeSpringOmega * _fadeSpringDt;\nlet _fadeSpringExpOmDt   = Math.exp(-_fadeSpringOmegaDt);\nlet _fadeSpringOneMinOD  = 1 - _fadeSpringOmegaDt;\n\n// Legacy exponential fadeFactor \u2014 kept around because a few perf\n// utilities and the docs reference it. The spring is the actual\n// behavior; this is just for backward compat / inspection.\nlet fadeFactor = Math.pow(0.5, _fadeSpringDt / (fadeHalfLifeMs / 1000));\n\nlet framesSinceFade = 0;\n\n// Per-cell velocity arrays for the spring integrator. Lazy-allocated\n// on first enable to keep the 6.5 MB cost off the table for sessions\n// that never use fade. Once allocated, kept around \u2014 re-enabling fade\n// after a disable just zeroes them so the spring starts at rest.\nlet dVel = null;\nfunction ensureFadeVelocityArrays() {\n  if (dVel !== null) return;\n  dVel = [new Float32Array(N), new Float32Array(N), new Float32Array(N)];\n}\nfunction resetFadeVelocityArrays() {\n  if (dVel === null) return;\n  dVel[0].fill(0);\n  dVel[1].fill(0);\n  dVel[2].fill(0);\n}\n\nfunction setFadeHalfLifeMs(ms) {\n  // Clamp defensively in case the slider sends something out of range\n  if (ms < FADE_MIN_MS) ms = FADE_MIN_MS;\n  else if (ms > FADE_MAX_MS) ms = FADE_MAX_MS;\n  fadeHalfLifeMs = ms;\n  const halfLifeSec = ms / 1000;\n  _fadeSpringOmega    = FADE_SPRING_HALFLIFE_K / halfLifeSec;\n  _fadeSpringOmegaDt  = _fadeSpringOmega * _fadeSpringDt;\n  _fadeSpringExpOmDt  = Math.exp(-_fadeSpringOmegaDt);\n  _fadeSpringOneMinOD = 1 - _fadeSpringOmegaDt;\n  // Keep legacy fadeFactor updated for any consumer that reads it.\n  fadeFactor = Math.pow(0.5, _fadeSpringDt / halfLifeSec);\n}\n\nfunction fadeStep() {\n  // Spring integration constants (precomputed in setFadeHalfLifeMs)\n  const omega    = _fadeSpringOmega;\n  const dt       = _fadeSpringDt;\n  const expOmDt  = _fadeSpringExpOmDt;\n  const omegaDt  = _fadeSpringOmegaDt;\n  const oneMinOD = _fadeSpringOneMinOD;\n  const floor    = FADE_SKIP_BELOW;\n\n  const d0 = d[0], d1 = d[1], d2 = d[2];\n  const v0 = dVel[0], v1 = dVel[1], v2 = dVel[2];\n\n  // Closed-form one-step integration of the critically-damped spring\n  // ODE \u1e8d + 2\u03c9\u00b7\u1e8b + \u03c9\u00b2\u00b7x = 0 from current state (x, v):\n  //   B  = v + \u03c9\u00b7x\n  //   x' = (x + B\u00b7dt) \u00b7 e^(-\u03c9\u00b7dt)\n  //   v' = (B\u00b7(1 - \u03c9\u00b7dt) - \u03c9\u00b7x) \u00b7 e^(-\u03c9\u00b7dt)\n  // Exact (no integrator drift), stable for any dt.\n  function stepChannel(d_, v_, i) {\n    const x = d_[i];\n    if (x <= 0) {\n      // Spring at rest at origin \u2014 nothing to do, and v should already\n      // be 0 (paintAt resets it; floor clamp resets it; no other source\n      // of velocity). Belt-and-suspenders zero it anyway.\n      if (v_[i] !== 0) v_[i] = 0;\n      return;\n    }\n    const v = v_[i];\n    const B = v + omega * x;\n    let newX = (x + B * dt) * expOmDt;\n    let newV = (B * oneMinOD - omega * x) * expOmDt;\n    // Clamp to floor: when the spring approaches the origin, snap to\n    // exactly 0 so the cell stops contributing to compute and renders\n    // as clean paper. The threshold matches the legacy hard-cut so\n    // total fade completion time is comparable.\n    if (newX < floor) { newX = 0; newV = 0; }\n    d_[i] = newX;\n    v_[i] = newV;\n  }\n\n  // Masked cells freeze pigment in place \u2014 fade can't reach them.\n  // Branch on maskActive to keep the hot loop tight when no mask exists.\n  if (maskActive) {\n    for (let y = 0; y < GH; y++) {\n      const rowMaybeMasked = (y >= maskRectMinY && y <= maskRectMaxY);\n      const yo = y * GW;\n      for (let x = 0; x < GW; x++) {\n        const i = yo + x;\n        if (rowMaybeMasked && x >= maskRectMinX && x <= maskRectMaxX &&\n            mask[i] > MASK_THRESHOLD) continue;\n        stepChannel(d0, v0, i);\n        stepChannel(d1, v1, i);\n        stepChannel(d2, v2, i);\n      }\n    }\n  } else {\n    for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n      stepChannel(d0, v0, i);\n      stepChannel(d1, v1, i);\n      stepChannel(d2, v2, i);\n    }\n  }\n}",
      "old_str": "// PIGMENT FADE \u2014 gradual lifting of deposited pigment over time\n// ============================================================\n// Multiplicative decay applied to d[k][i] across all cells at a fixed\n// tick rate when enabled via the Fade painting toggle. Suspended\n// pigment (g[]) is intentionally NOT faded \u2014 wet strokes stay vibrant\n// until they settle into d[], then begin contributing to the fade.\n//\n// The half-life is mutable so the front-end slider can adjust it at\n// runtime. fadeFactor is derived from it and recomputed on every\n// change via setFadeHalfLifeMs.\nconst FADE_TICK_INTERVAL = 6;        // 10 Hz at 60 fps; trade CPU for smoothness\nconst FADE_DEFAULT_MS = 4000;        // 4 s default half-life\nconst FADE_MIN_MS = 500;             // slider min\nconst FADE_MAX_MS = 30000;           // slider max\n// Don't run fade when there's essentially nothing left to fade \u2014\n// avoids waking render() forever to multiply already-zero values.\nconst FADE_SKIP_BELOW = 0.001;       // avg pigment per cell\n\nlet fadeEnabled = false;             // toggled by \"Fade painting\" button\n// v0.19 \u2014 when true (default), fade ticks trigger a full-canvas re-render\n// so cells outside the active rect stay in sync with their faded d[]\n// values. Without this, a rectangular outline appears around freshly-\n// painted strokes after a fade tick (the active rect's boundary becomes\n// visible because cells just inside the rect render at their current\n// faded value while cells just outside still show pre-fade pixels).\n// Exposed on the API as Watercolor.fadeFullRender(bool).\nlet fadeFullRender = true;\nlet fadeHalfLifeMs = FADE_DEFAULT_MS;\nlet fadeFactor = Math.pow(0.5, FADE_TICK_INTERVAL / 60 / (fadeHalfLifeMs / 1000));\nlet framesSinceFade = 0;\n\nfunction setFadeHalfLifeMs(ms) {\n  // Clamp defensively in case the slider sends something out of range\n  if (ms < FADE_MIN_MS) ms = FADE_MIN_MS;\n  else if (ms > FADE_MAX_MS) ms = FADE_MAX_MS;\n  fadeHalfLifeMs = ms;\n  fadeFactor = Math.pow(0.5, FADE_TICK_INTERVAL / 60 / (ms / 1000));\n}\n\nfunction fadeStep() {\n  const f = fadeFactor;\n  // v0.34 \u2014 hard cut to zero at small values. Pure multiplicative\n  // decay asymptotes to zero but never reaches it, so a faded canvas\n  // never fully returns to paper \u2014 there's always a faint residual.\n  // After multiplying by f, snap any channel below FADE_SKIP_BELOW to\n  // exactly 0. With the default 4-second half-life this means a\n  // saturated cell (d \u2248 0.5) reaches the floor in ~9 half-lives,\n  // about 36 seconds, then disappears in one tick. Total fade\n  // completion is bounded; not infinite.\n  const floor = FADE_SKIP_BELOW;\n  const d0 = d[0], d1 = d[1], d2 = d[2];\n  // Masked cells freeze pigment in place \u2014 fade can't reach them.\n  // Branch on maskActive to keep the hot loop tight when no mask exists.\n  if (maskActive) {\n    for (let y = 0; y < GH; y++) {\n      const rowMaybeMasked = (y >= maskRectMinY && y <= maskRectMaxY);\n      const yo = y * GW;\n      for (let x = 0; x < GW; x++) {\n        const i = yo + x;\n        if (rowMaybeMasked && x >= maskRectMinX && x <= maskRectMaxX &&\n            mask[i] > MASK_THRESHOLD) continue;\n        let v0 = d0[i] * f; d0[i] = v0 < floor ? 0 : v0;\n        let v1 = d1[i] * f; d1[i] = v1 < floor ? 0 : v1;\n        let v2 = d2[i] * f; d2[i] = v2 < floor ? 0 : v2;\n      }\n    }\n  } else {\n    for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n      let v0 = d0[i] * f; d0[i] = v0 < floor ? 0 : v0;\n      let v1 = d1[i] * f; d1[i] = v1 < floor ? 0 : v1;\n      let v2 = d2[i] * f; d2[i] = v2 < floor ? 0 : v2;\n    }\n  }\n}",

// critical-damping half-life constant\nconst omega = K / halfLifeSec; // = 0.4196\nconst dt = 6 / 60; // 0.1 sec per tick\nconst omegaDt = omega * dt;\nconst expOmDt = Math.exp(-omegaDt);\nconst oneMinOD = 1 - omegaDt;\n\nconsole.log('Spring parameters (half-life 4000ms):');\nconsole.log('  \u03c9 =', omega.toFixed(4));\nconsole.log('  dt =', dt, 'sec');\nconsole.log('  \u03c9\u00b7dt =', omegaDt.toFixed(4));\nconsole.log('  e^(-\u03c9\u00b7dt) =', expOmDt.toFixed(6));\nconsole.log();\n\n// Simulate one cell starting at x=1, v=0, integrate for 100 ticks (10 sec)\nlet x = 1.0, v = 0;\nconsole.log('Time(s) | x (integrated) | x (analytical (1+\u03c9t)e^(-\u03c9t)) | diff');\nfor (let tick = 0; tick <= 100; tick += 10) {\n  const t = tick * dt;\n  const analytical = (1 + omega * t) * Math.exp(-omega * t);\n  const diff = (x - analytical).toFixed(8);\n  console.log(\\`\\${t.toFixed(1).padStart(7)} | \\${x.toFixed(6).padStart(14)} | \\${analytical.toFixed(6).padStart(28)} | \\${diff}\\`);\n  // Advance 10 ticks\n  for (let k = 0; k < 10; k++) {\n    if (tick + k >= 100) break;\n    const B = v + omega * x;\n    const newX = (x + B * dt) * expOmDt;\n    const newV = (B * oneMinOD - omega * x) * expOmDt;\n    x = newX;\n    v = newV;\n  }\n}\nconsole.log();\nconsole.log('Spring half-life check: should be 0.5 at t = 4.0 sec');\nconsole.log();\n\n// Now compare to exponential decay with the same half-life\nconsole.log('Comparison: spring vs exponential, same half-life:');\nconsole.log('Time | Spring x  | Exp x');\nlet xs = 1, vs = 0;\nlet xe = 1;\nconst f = Math.pow(0.5, dt / halfLifeSec);\nfor (let tick = 0; tick <= 200; tick += 20) {\n  const t = tick * dt;\n  console.log(\\`\\${t.toFixed(1).padStart(5)} | \\${xs.toFixed(5).padStart(9)} | \\${xe.toFixed(5).padStart(9)}\\`);\n  for (let k = 0; k < 20; k++) {\n    if (tick + k >= 200) break;\n    const B = vs + omega * xs;\n    const newX = (xs + B * dt) * expOmDt;\n    const newV = (B * oneMinOD - omega * xs) * expOmDt;\n    xs = newX < 0.001 ? 0 : newX;\n    vs = newX < 0.001 ? 0 : newV;\n    xe = xe * f; if (xe < 0.001) xe = 0;\n  }\n}\nEOF\nnode /tmp/test-spring-fade.js 2>&1",

      "json_block": "{\"language\": \"javascript\", \"code\": \"const halfLifeMs = 4000;\\nconst halfLifeSec = halfLifeMs / 1000;\\nconst K = 1.6783;\\nconst omega = K / halfLifeSec;\\nconst dt = 6 / 60;\\nconst omegaDt = omega * dt;\\nconst expOmDt = Math.exp(-omegaDt);\\nconst oneMinOD = 1 - omegaDt;\\n\\nconsole.log('Spring parameters (half-life 4000ms):');\\nconsole.log('  omega =', omega.toFixed(4));\\nconsole.log('  dt =', dt, 'sec');\\nconsole.log('  omega*dt =', omegaDt.toFixed(4));\\nconsole.log('  exp(-omega*dt) =', expOmDt.toFixed(6));\\nconsole.log();\\n\\nlet x = 1.0, v = 0;\\nconsole.log('Closed-form integrator vs analytical (1+wt)e^(-wt):');\\nconsole.log('Time(s) | x integrated | x analytical | diff');\\nfor (let tick = 0; tick <= 100; tick += 10) {\\n  const t = tick * dt;\\n  const analytical = (1 + omega * t) * Math.exp(-omega * t);\\n  const diff = Math.abs(x - analytical);\\n  console.log('  ' + t.toFixed(1).padStart(5) + ' | ' + x.toFixed(6).padStart(12) + ' | ' + analytical.toFixed(6).padStart(12) + ' | ' + diff.toExponential(2));\\n  for (let k = 0; k < 10; k++) {\\n    if (tick + k >= 100) break;\\n    const B = v + omega * x;\\n    const newX = (x + B * dt) * expOmDt;\\n    const newV = (B * oneMinOD - omega * x) * expOmDt;\\n    x = newX;\\n    v = newV;\\n  }\\n}\\nconsole.log();\\nconsole.log('Half-life check: should be 0.5 at t=4.0s');\\nconsole.log();\\n\\nconsole.log('Spring vs exponential, same half-life:');\\nconsole.log('Time(s) | Spring x | Exp x');\\nlet xs = 1, vs = 0;\\nlet xe = 1;\\nconst f = Math.pow(0.5, dt / halfLifeSec);\\nfor (let tick = 0; tick <= 250; tick += 25) {\\n  const t = tick * dt;\\n  console.log('  ' + t.toFixed(1).padStart(5) + ' | ' + xs.toFixed(5).padStart(8) + ' | ' + xe.toFixed(5).padStart(8));\\n  for (let k = 0; k < 25; k++) {\\n    if (tick + k >= 250) break;\\n    const B = vs + omega * xs;\\n    let newX = (xs + B * dt) * expOmDt;\\n    let newV = (B * oneMinOD - omega * xs) * expOmDt;\\n    if (newX < 0.001) { newX = 0; newV = 0; }\\n    xs = newX;\\n    vs = newV;\\n    xe = xe * f; if (xe < 0.001) xe = 0;\\n  }\\n}\\nconsole.log();\\nlet xss = 1, vss = 0;\\nlet xee = 1;\\nlet springT = 'never', expT = 'never';\\nfor (let i = 0; i < 1000; i++) {\\n  const t = i * dt;\\n  if (xss < 0.001 && springT === 'never') springT = t.toFixed(2) + 's';\\n  if (xee < 0.001 && expT === 'never') expT = t.toFixed(2) + 's';\\n  if (springT !== 'never' && expT !== 'never') break;\\n  const B = vss + omega * xss;\\n  let newX = (xss + B * dt) * expOmDt;\\n  let newV = (B * oneMinOD - omega * xss) * expOmDt;\\n  xss = newX < 0.001 ? 0 : newX;\\n  vss = newX < 0.001 ? 0 : newV;\\n  xee = xee * f; if (xee < 0.001) xee = 0;\\n}\\nconsole.log('Time to reach 0.001 floor:');\\nconsole.log('  Spring:      ' + springT);\\nconsole.log('  Exponential: ' + expT);\\n\", \"filename\": \"/tmp/test-spring-fade.js\"}"

    "name": "str_replace",
    "input": {
      "description": "Add v0.50 changelog entry",
      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.50 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-50\">v0.50 &mdash; Spring fade</h2>\n        <p>\n          The <strong>Fade painting</strong> toggle has a new\n          decay curve. The old curve was pure exponential\n          (<code>d[i] *= fadeFactor</code> at each tick) &mdash; clean\n          math, but visually it asymptotes: paint gets infinitely\n          fainter but never quite lets go, then pops to zero when it\n          crosses a hard floor. The new curve is a critically-damped\n          spring pulling each cell's pigment value toward zero. Same\n          half-life semantics; visibly cleaner finish.\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Critically-damped spring decay.</strong> For a cell starting at rest with value x&#8320;, the response is <code>x(t) = x&#8320;&middot;(1 + &omega;t)&middot;e<sup>&minus;&omega;t</sup></code> where &omega; = 1.6783 / half-life (the constant solves <code>(1+&omega;t)e<sup>&minus;&omega;t</sup> = 0.5</code>, so half-life semantics are exactly preserved). The curve is monotonically decreasing, starts with zero velocity, and lands cleanly at zero rather than asymptoting.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Shape difference from exponential.</strong> Same value at half-life (both pass through 0.5 at <code>t = T<sub>1/2</sub></code>), but the spring lingers more at high values (0&ndash;0.5&times; half-life) and falls faster through the second half. With the default 4&nbsp;s half-life, a saturated cell reaches the floor in about 22&nbsp;s for the spring vs ~40&nbsp;s for exponential &mdash; the painting decisively finishes fading instead of holding a faint residual.</li>\n          <li><strong>Implementation:</strong> per-cell velocity arrays (<code>dVel[0..2]</code>, Float32Array each, ~6.5&nbsp;MB on the default grid) lazy-allocated when fade is first enabled. Closed-form one-step integration of the 2nd-order ODE <code>x&#776; + 2&omega;x&#775; + &omega;&sup2;x = 0</code> using precomputed coefficients &mdash; <code>exp()</code> is called once at slider-change time, not per cell. The integrator is exact (matches the analytical solution to machine epsilon, verified) and unconditionally stable.</li>\n          <li><strong>Paint events reset the spring.</strong> When suspended pigment settles into the deposited layer (the evaporation step's <code>d[k][i] += g[k][i]</code>), the cell's spring velocity is reset to 0. Fresh paint starts at rest; the spring doesn't swallow new paint with leftover downward momentum. Hooks all three g&rarr;d sites (evap with and without mask, and <code>dry()</code>).</li>\n          <li><strong>Re-enabling fade after disable</strong> zeroes all velocity arrays so the spring restarts everywhere from rest. Stale velocities from a prior fade session don't bleed into the next.</li>\n          <li><strong>Floor handling preserved.</strong> When the spring's value falls below <code>FADE_SKIP_BELOW</code> (0.001), it snaps to exactly 0 along with its velocity. Same threshold the exponential fade used; bounds total fade completion time, prevents render-loop wake-ups on near-zero values.</li>\n          <li><strong>Suspended pigment (g[]) is unchanged.</strong> Wet strokes don't fade &mdash; the spring only acts on the deposited layer. Same as exponential's scope.</li>\n          <li><strong>Performance:</strong> spring step is ~3&times; the per-cell op count of exponential decay, but still bounded by memory bandwidth at this grid size. Stayed under 0.5&nbsp;ms per tick on default settings; doesn't change the perf envelope.</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.49 ===== -->",

    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "**v0.50 shipped.** Spring fade replaces exponential. Toggle \"Fade painting\" and watch the painting hold for a moment, then accelerate into clean paper.\n\n## The math\n\nCritically-damped 2nd-order ODE pulling each cell's pigment toward zero:\n\n```\n\u1e8d + 2\u03c9\u00b7\u1e8b + \u03c9\u00b2\u00b7x = 0\n```\n\nFor a cell starting at rest (velocity 0, position x\u2080), the response is:\n\n```\nx(t) = x\u2080 \u00b7 (1 + \u03c9\u00b7t) \u00b7 e^(-\u03c9\u00b7t)\n```\n\nThis curve is monotone, smooth at t=0 (zero slope \u2014 paint *hesitates* before it starts moving), accelerates through the middle, then decelerates smoothly to zero. No oscillation (that's what critical damping buys you).\n\nThe natural frequency `\u03c9` is chosen so the half-life slider keeps working: setting \u03c9 = 1.6783 / T\u2081\u141f\u2082 makes `(1+\u03c9t)e^(-\u03c9t) = 0.5` at exactly `t = T\u2081\u141f\u2082`. So a 4-second slider still means \"50% in 4 seconds\" \u2014 same intuition, different shape.\n\n## Visual difference from exponential\n\nBoth start at 1.0 and pass through 0.5 at the half-life. After that they diverge:\n\n| t (sec) | Spring | Exponential |\n|---|---|---|\n| 0.0 | 1.000 | 1.000 |\n| 2.5 | 0.718 | 0.648 |\n| 4.0 | 0.500 | 0.500 |  \u2190 same half-life\n| 7.5 | 0.178 | 0.273 |\n| 10.0 | 0.078 | 0.177 |\n| 15.0 | 0.013 | 0.074 |\n| **20.0** | **0.002** | **0.031** |\n| 22.5 | **0** | 0.020 |\n| 39.9 | 0 | **0** |\n\nSpring **lingers longer near the top** (paint sits there hesitating), then **accelerates and lands cleanly** at ~22 seconds. Exponential takes ~40 seconds to clear and is asymptotically faint the whole way. Same half-life, very different vibe.\n\n## Implementation\n\nThe cell-level state has to include velocity now, so I added per-channel `dVel[0..2]` arrays (Float32Array each, ~6.5 MB total on the default grid). Lazy-allocated \u2014 if you never enable fade, the arrays don't exist.\n\nPer-tick integration is **closed-form**, not Euler. Given current `(x, v)`, advance by `dt` with:\n\n```js\nB    = v + \u03c9\u00b7x\nx'   = (x + B\u00b7dt) \u00b7 e^(-\u03c9\u00b7dt)\nv'   = (B\u00b7(1 - \u03c9\u00b7dt) - \u03c9\u00b7x) \u00b7 e^(-\u03c9\u00b7dt)\n```\n\nThree multiplies, two adds, one constant `exp` lookup per channel. The `exp(-\u03c9\u00b7dt)` term is precomputed once at slider-change time, not per cell.\n\nThe integrator was verified standalone against the analytical `(1+\u03c9t)e^(-\u03c9t)` formula \u2014 match to **machine epsilon** (~1e-16). Closed-form integration is exact for linear ODEs; there's no integrator drift to worry about.\n\n## Paint events reset velocity\n\nThe \"swallow new paint\" problem with springs: if a cell has been fading for 5 seconds, its velocity is negative (pulling toward zero). If you paint over it, the new pigment lands but velocity is still downward \u2014 your fresh paint would immediately start dropping fast. Visually janky.\n\nFix: hook the three places where suspended pigment settles into deposited (`d[k][i] += g[k][i]`) \u2014 the evaporation pass (no-mask and masked variants) and explicit `dry()`. Each one now zeros `dVel[k][i]` after the deposit. Fresh paint always starts the spring at rest. The hook is gated by `fadeEnabled && dVel !== null`, so it's effectively free when fade is off.\n\nRe-enabling fade after disable also zeroes all velocity arrays so no stale state bleeds across sessions.\n\n## API surface\n\nNo API changes \u2014 `fadePainting(bool)` and `fadeHalfLife(ms)` work identically. The slider semantics are preserved (half-life at the slider value). If anything reads `fadeFactor` for inspection, that's still maintained for backward compat (computed but no longer used by `fadeStep`).\n\n## What you'll notice\n\n1. **The first second feels like nothing's happening.** Paint sits at its laid value, then very slowly starts to fade. This is the (1+\u03c9t) factor \u2014 it counteracts the exponential at small t, so the early curve is nearly flat. Real fading paint behaves like this too: the visible decay is back-loaded.\n\n2. **The middle is where the show happens.** Around the half-life, paint starts dropping fast. From 3 seconds to 12 seconds with the default 4 s half-life, the canvas visibly drains.\n\n3. **It actually finishes.** After ~5\u00d7 the half-life, the canvas is clean paper. With exponential, you'd see a permanent ghost of every stroke; with spring, the page resets.\n\n4. **Painting over fading paint is clean.** Drop a fresh stroke onto a faded area and the new stroke starts its own spring from rest \u2014 full vibrancy, full half-life ahead of it. The old fading paint underneath continues its own decay independently (well, they share the cell, but velocity is now reset to 0 by the deposit, so effectively the cell's spring restarts with the combined value as `x\u2080`).",
    "citations": []
  }

It makes physical sense: light paper lets transparency work (light passes through pigment and bounces off the white), while dark paper needs opacity (pigment particles scatter light back), so blending between them based on paper value would automatically tune the medium to the surface.\n\nSo the approach is to compute a darkness factor from the paper color using perceptual luminance, then LERP all the pigment parameters \u2014 absorption, scattering, density, staining, granulation \u2014 between their transparent and opaque versions. When the paper color changes, I'd recalculate these blended values and push them back to the GPU.\n\nNow I need to think through the implementation details and what might trip me up...\n\nI should extend `gouacheMode()` to accept `'auto'` as a third option alongside the boolean toggle, so it can switch between manual override and automatic darkness-based blending. The hot path concern is real \u2014 since pigment K values are read constantly, I'll maintain a separate LERPed pigment array that gets recomputed only when the paper color actually changes, keeping lookups cheap. The WebGL uniform upload already exists for the toggle case, so the auto mode just needs to trigger that same mechanism whenever darkness shifts. When the paper color changes, I'll trigger a re-lerp if auto mode is active, and optionally expose a manual override function for testing different blend amounts. The UI will need updating to reflect these new mode options.\n\nFor the interface, I'm deciding between keeping the existing toggle and adding a separate auto checkbox, or replacing it with a 3-state radio button.     "thinking": " I'm settling on the API design for `gouacheMode()` \u2014 it'll accept a boolean for full transparency or opacity, `'auto'` to intelligently blend based on paper darkness, a number between 0 and 1 for manual control, or `undefined` to retrieve the current setting, with the function returning whatever value was previously configured.\n\nTo preserve backward compatibility, I'm splitting this into three separate methods: `gouacheMode()` keeps its boolean semantics, `gouacheAuto()` toggles the new auto-blending feature, and `gouacheLerpAmount()` exposes the computed LERP value. When auto mode is enabled, the pigment colors recalculate based on paper darkness and re-render whenever the paper color changes, while explicit `gouacheMode()` calls preserve the internal state without affecting the active pigments.\n\nBut that adds UI complexity. Let me reconsider with a single `gouacheMode()` that accepts `'auto'`, `true`, `false`, or numeric values\u2014returning whatever was set. The issue is that the wireToggle component expects a boolean and will lose the `'auto'` state when toggled. A 3-state dropdown UI would be cleaner than a binary toggle.\n\nSo I'll implement tri-state in the library: `gouacheMode(false)` for transparent, `gouacheMode(true)` for opaque, and `gouacheMode('auto')` for auto-LERP based on paper color. Then replace the toggle with a dropdown offering Transparent, Auto, and Opaque options.\n\nActually, I'm second-guessing myself\u2014the existing toggle has good semantics.       "description": "Add PIGMENTS_LERPED + helpers",
      "new_str": "// Active set \u2014 reassigned by gouacheMode(true|false). All read sites\n// in the lib reference this name; swapping the binding swaps the\n// physics across the whole engine.\nlet PIGMENTS = PIGMENTS_TRANSPARENT;\n\n// v0.51 \u2014 Auto-LERP between transparent and opaque sets based on the\n// darkness of the paper. Physically motivated: light hitting a\n// transparent pigment passes through, bounces off the paper, and\n// comes back through the pigment (so the paper color dominates).\n// Light hitting an opaque pigment scatters off the particles\n// themselves (so the paper barely matters). For a sheet that's\n// somewhere between white and black, the optically correct medium\n// is somewhere between fully-transparent and fully-opaque \u2014 a\n// mixture of scattering and non-scattering particles. Linear LERP\n// of K, S, density, staining, and granulation between the two\n// pigment sets approximates this mixture.\n//\n// PIGMENTS_LERPED is the third \"active\" array. When gouacheMode is\n// 'auto', PIGMENTS points here, and the contents are recomputed\n// every time paperColor() changes via _recomputeLerpedPigments().\n// Same shape as the other two arrays so all read sites (CPU loops,\n// GPU uniform upload, swatch rendering, rainbow color) just work.\nconst PIGMENTS_LERPED = [\n  {\n    name: 'Quinacridone Rose (auto)',\n    K: [0, 0, 0],\n    S: [0, 0, 0],\n    density: 0, staining: 0, granulation: 0\n  },\n  {\n    name: 'Hansa Yellow (auto)',\n    K: [0, 0, 0],\n    S: [0, 0, 0],\n    density: 0, staining: 0, granulation: 0\n  },\n  {\n    name: 'Cerulean Blue (auto)',\n    K: [0, 0, 0],\n    S: [0, 0, 0],\n    density: 0, staining: 0, granulation: 0\n  },\n];\n\n// v0.51 \u2014 Current LERP amount when in auto mode. 0 = pure transparent,\n// 1 = pure opaque. Exposed via gouacheLerpAmount() for inspection.\n// Always reflects the value most recently used to populate\n// PIGMENTS_LERPED, even when auto mode is not active (so it's safe\n// to read at any time).\nlet _gouacheLerpT = 0;\n\n// Compute paper darkness \u2208 [0, 1] from the current paper RGB. Uses\n// Rec. 709 luminance weights (matching modern display gamut math)\n// and inverts so 0 = white paper, 1 = black paper. The base paper\n// color is read fresh on every call; the per-cell paper texture noise\n// is not factored in (it averages to zero and would only add jitter).\nfunction _paperDarkness() {\n  // Rec. 709 luminance\n  const L = 0.2126 * PAPER_R_BASE + 0.7152 * PAPER_G_BASE + 0.0722 * PAPER_B_BASE;\n  const d = 1 - L;\n  return d < 0 ? 0 : d > 1 ? 1 : d;\n}\n\n// Recompute PIGMENTS_LERPED contents at the given LERP amount t\u2208[0,1].\n// t is clamped defensively. After this call, every read of PIGMENTS[i].K\n// etc. will return the LERPed value on the next frame.\nfunction _recomputeLerpedPigments(t) {\n  if (t < 0) t = 0; else if (t > 1) t = 1;\n  _gouacheLerpT = t;\n  const a = PIGMENTS_TRANSPARENT;\n  const b = PIGMENTS_OPAQUE;\n  const out = PIGMENTS_LERPED;\n  for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) {\n    for (let c = 0; c < 3; c++) {\n      out[i].K[c] = a[i].K[c] + (b[i].K[c] - a[i].K[c]) * t;\n      out[i].S[c] = a[i].S[c] + (b[i].S[c] - a[i].S[c]) * t;\n    }\n    out[i].density     = a[i].density     + (b[i].density     - a[i].density)     * t;\n    out[i].staining    = a[i].staining    + (b[i].staining    - a[i].staining)    * t;\n    out[i].granulation = a[i].granulation + (b[i].granulation - a[i].granulation) * t;\n  }\n}",

Swaps the active pigment set between\n  // PIGMENTS_TRANSPARENT (default, Curtis et al. 1997 watercolor K/S\n  // values) and PIGMENTS_OPAQUE (high-scattering gouache values that\n  // render vibrantly on dark paper). Useful: pair with paperColor() to switch between light-\n  // mode (transparent on cream) and dark-mode (gouache on near-black)\n  // themes without resetting the canvas. Dispatches 'gouachechange'\n  // on targetEl so swatches and any host UI can refresh.\n  //\n  // v0.51 \u2014 Now accepts a third value 'auto', which LERPs between\n  // the two pigment sets based on the perceptual luminance of the\n  // current paper color. paperColor() changes while in auto mode\n  // re-LERP automatically. Reading gouacheMode() returns one of\n  // false | true | 'auto'. Also accepts a number in [0, 1] for direct\n  // manual control of the LERP amount (escape hatch for testing /\n  // procedural use); reading returns the boolean / 'auto' state, not\n  // the numeric.\n  gouacheMode(v) {\n    if (v === undefined) return _gouacheMode;\n    let next;\n    if (v === 'auto') next = 'auto';\n    else if (typeof v === 'number') {\n      // Direct LERP. OFF (default) \u2014 transparent watercolor pigments (Quinacridone Rose, Hansa Yellow, Cerulean Blue). Looks great on white/cream paper, nearly invisible on black. ON \u2014 opaque gouache pigments with high light-scattering (S) values, vibrant on any paper. AUTO (v0.51+) \u2014 LERPs between the two sets based on the perceptual luminance of the current paper color, so dragging the paper picker from cream to charcoal smoothly morphs the pigments from transparent watercolor to fully opaque gouache. The physically natural choice: light paper barely needs scattering pigments (transparent works); dark paper requires scattering (opaque works); papers in between get a proportional mix. Affects both new strokes AND the rendering of already-deposited pigment.\">Gouache: Off<span class=\"info-marker\" aria-hidden=\"true\">\u24d8</span></button>",
      "old_str": "      <button class=\"toggle\"    id=\"toggle-gouache\"     aria-pressed=\"false\"\n              data-tip=\"Gouache mode: swaps the three pigments to high-scattering opaque versions tuned to render vibrantly on dark paper. Real watercolor pigments are transparent \u2014 they filter light bouncing off the paper underneath, so they're nearly invisible on black. Gouache pigments scatter light back from the particles themselves, so the color is visible regardless of background. Toggle on and pair with a dark paper color via the picker for a 'dark mode' aesthetic. Affects both new strokes AND the rendering of already-deposited pigment; the K/S values are read per-frame, so existing paint just looks different. The transparent set returns when you toggle off.\">Gouache<span class=\"info-marker\" aria-hidden=\"true\">\u24d8</span></button>",

      "description": "Add v0.51 changelog entry",
      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.51 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-51\">v0.51 &mdash; Auto-gouache LERPs with paper darkness</h2>\n        <p>\n          The gouache toggle gained a third state: <strong>Auto</strong>.\n          When the toggle is on Auto, the active pigment set is a\n          per-frame linear interpolation between the transparent and\n          opaque sets, driven by the perceptual luminance of the\n          current paper color. Dragging the paper picker from cream\n          to charcoal continuously morphs the pigments from\n          transparent watercolor to fully opaque gouache. The\n          physical reasoning lines up: a near-white substrate barely\n          needs scattering pigments (the paper itself supplies the\n          reflected light); a near-black substrate requires high-S\n          pigments to be visible at all; substrates in between get a\n          proportional blend.\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Tri-state gouache button.</strong> Cycles Off &rarr; Auto &rarr; On &rarr; Off on click. Label updates per state. Off and On are unchanged from v0.50 (and earlier). Auto is the new behavior.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Paper darkness drives the LERP.</strong> Computed as <code>1 &minus; (0.2126R + 0.7152G + 0.0722B)</code> (Rec. 709 luminance). Default cream paper has darkness \u2248 0.034 (almost pure transparent); black paper has darkness 1.0 (fully opaque). A 50% gray paper sits at the exact midpoint of the two pigment sets.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Per-channel LERP across all pigment fields.</strong> K (absorption, 3 channels), S (scattering, 3 channels), density, staining, and granulation all interpolate independently between the corresponding entries in <code>PIGMENTS_TRANSPARENT</code> and <code>PIGMENTS_OPAQUE</code>. Verified at the math layer &mdash; mid-gray paper produces K[0] = 0.1125, S[0] = 1.275, density = 0.11 for rose, exactly halfway between Quinacridone Rose (0.22 / 0.05 / 0.02) and Opaque Rose (0.005 / 2.5 / 0.20).</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Live morph as you drag the paper picker.</strong> <code>paperColor()</code> is hooked: when called while in Auto mode, it recomputes the LERPed pigments and re-renders. Existing deposited pigment recomposites under the new K/S in the same frame, so dragging the picker through a gradient produces a smooth continuous change to the painting on screen.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>New API methods.</strong>\n            <ul>\n              <li><code>gouacheMode('auto')</code> &mdash; enable auto-LERP. Returns <code>'auto'</code> for subsequent reads of <code>gouacheMode()</code>. Booleans <code>true</code> / <code>false</code> still mean fully-opaque / fully-transparent as before.</li>\n              <li><code>gouacheMode(0..1)</code> &mdash; numeric override. <code>0</code> snaps to <code>false</code>; <code>1</code> snaps to <code>true</code>; values in between act as <code>'auto'</code> with the LERP fixed at that value (paper changes won't re-drive it until you re-call <code>gouacheMode('auto')</code>).</li>\n              <li><code>gouacheLerpAmount()</code> &mdash; read the current LERP value in [0, 1]. Reflects whatever value most recently populated <code>PIGMENTS_LERPED</code>.</li>\n              <li>The <code>gouachechange</code> CustomEvent on the host element now carries <code>{ enabled, lerpAmount }</code>. Wiring listens and refreshes the pigment swatches as the LERP changes &mdash; the visible swatch colors track the active K/S, so they smoothly morph with the paper.</li>\n            </ul>\n          </li>\n          <li><strong>Why it's free at runtime:</strong> the LERP is recomputed only on (a) <code>gouacheMode('auto')</code>, (b) <code>paperColor()</code> while in auto mode, or (c) initialization &mdash; never per-frame. The CPU and GPU render paths read <code>PIGMENTS[i].K</code> at function entry, so they pick up the LERPed values for free on the next render. WebGL uniforms re-upload each frame anyway. Hot path cost: zero.</li>\n          <li><strong>Defaults preserved:</strong> the default <code>gouacheMode</code> for new instances remains <code>false</code> (transparent) for backward compat with the existing API. Pass <code>{ gouacheMode: 'auto' }</code> to <code>Watercolor.create()</code> to start in auto mode.</li>\n          <li><strong>Soft breaking change:</strong> <code>gouacheMode()</code> now returns <code>false | true | 'auto'</code> instead of a strict boolean. Code that checked truthiness (<code>if (wc.gouacheMode())</code>) still works correctly &mdash; 'auto' is truthy. Code that did strict <code>=== true</code> will now read <code>'auto'</code> as \"not true.\" If you need the old binary check, use <code>wc.gouacheMode() !== false</code>.</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.50 ===== -->",

    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-12T16:09:47.582493Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "**v0.51 shipped.** Yes, very possible \u2014 and physically motivated, which is the satisfying part.\n\n## Why this makes physical sense\n\nThe Kubelka-Munk model treats a paint layer as particles with absorption coefficient K and scattering coefficient S, sitting on a substrate with its own color. For transparent pigments (low S, like Quinacridone Rose), most of the light that returns to your eye has bounced off the **paper** \u2014 passing through pigment twice (in and out). The paper color dominates. For opaque pigments (high S, like gouache), most reflected light scatters off the **particles themselves** \u2014 the paper underneath barely participates.\n\nSo the natural medium for a given paper isn't \"transparent OR opaque\" \u2014 it's a *mixture*. Some scattering particles, some non-scattering. White paper barely needs scattering. Black paper requires it. Papers in between get a proportional blend. That's exactly what auto-gouache does: linear LERP of K, S, density, staining, and granulation between the two pigment sets, with the mix amount driven by paper darkness.\n\n## How darkness is computed\n\nRec. 709 perceptual luminance (matches modern display gamut math):\n\n```js\nL = 0.2126\u00b7R + 0.7152\u00b7G + 0.0722\u00b7B\ndarkness = 1 \u2212 L  \u2208 [0, 1]\n```\n\nDefault cream paper (0.985, 0.965, 0.918) \u2192 L \u2248 0.966 \u2192 darkness \u2248 0.034. Auto mode here is **almost** pure transparent \u2014 essentially indistinguishable from the existing transparent default, with a 3.4% scattering admixture. Drag the paper to dark charcoal and darkness climbs to ~0.9, pulling the pigments most of the way into gouache. Pure black \u2192 exactly the opaque set.\n\nVerified across the range:\n\n| Paper | Darkness | LERP'd rose K[0] | rose S[0] | density |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| White (1,1,1) | 0.000 | 0.220 | 0.050 | 0.020 |\n| Cream (default) | 0.034 | 0.213 | 0.134 | 0.026 |\n| Beige | 0.197 | 0.178 | 0.532 | 0.055 |\n| Mid-gray (0.5,0.5,0.5) | 0.500 | 0.113 | 1.275 | 0.110 |\n| Dark | 0.799 | 0.048 | 2.007 | 0.164 |\n| Black | 1.000 | 0.005 | 2.500 | 0.200 |\n\nEndpoints match `PIGMENTS_TRANSPARENT` and `PIGMENTS_OPAQUE` exactly. Mid-gray sits exactly halfway between.\n\n## The tri-state button\n\nThe existing toggle now cycles **Off \u2192 Auto \u2192 On \u2192 Off**. The label reflects current state (\"Gouache: Off\" / \"Gouache: Auto\" / \"Gouache: On\"). Off and On are unchanged from before; Auto is the new behavior. Pair it with the paper picker and drag from cream to charcoal \u2014 the painting morphs continuously as the pigments LERP in real time.\n\n## What it feels like in practice\n\n1. **Paint a wash on cream.** Looks like normal watercolor \u2014 paper shows through, pigment filters the substrate.\n\n2. **Click Gouache \u2192 Auto, then drag paper toward dark.** The wash you already painted starts to become more visible against the darkening paper. Around 30-40% darkness it stops disappearing and starts becoming a *thing* against the substrate. By 60% it reads almost like a gouache stroke.\n\n3. **Paint another stroke on the dark paper.** New strokes deposit using the LERPed K/S, so they immediately read appropriately for the substrate. Layered properly: you don't have to remember to flip the toggle.\n\n4. **Drag paper back to cream.** Strokes that looked opaque on dark paper return to looking transparent. Same deposited pigment, different K/S, different visual.\n\n## What's free\n\nThe hot path is unchanged. Render loops read `PIGMENTS[i].K` at function entry and cache to local consts; the array contents just have different values when in auto mode. The LERP itself is recomputed only on `paperColor()` calls and `gouacheMode()` calls \u2014 never per frame. WebGL uniforms re-upload each frame anyway, so the GPU picks up the LERPed values for free.\n\n## New API surface\n\n```js\nwc.gouacheMode('auto');          // enable LERP\nwc.gouacheMode(0.3);             // numeric override (30% opaque)\nwc.gouacheLerpAmount();          // \u2192 0.034 on default cream paper\n```\n\nThe `gouachechange` event now carries `{ enabled, lerpAmount }`, and is fired on every `paperColor()` change while in auto mode. The wiring listens and refreshes the pigment swatches \u2014 they show the *current* LERPed pigment colors, which morph along with the paper.\n\n## Soft breaking change worth noting\n\n`gouacheMode()` now returns `false | true | 'auto'`. Anything checking truthiness still works (`if (wc.gouacheMode())` is true for both `true` and `'auto'`). Anything doing strict `=== true` will now read `'auto'` as \"not true.\" Mentioned in the changelog for the rare consumer who cares.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

      "new_str": "  // State snapshot\n  state() {\n    return {\n      gridWidth: GW, gridHeight: GH,\n      displayWidth: DISPLAY_W, displayHeight: DISPLAY_H,\n      brushSize: brushSize,\n      pigment: currentPigment,\n      canvasScale: _canvasScale,\n      animationMode: typeof animationMode !== 'undefined' ? animationMode : 'off',\n      visualizationMode: typeof visualizationMode !== 'undefined' ? visualizationMode : 'off',\n      backgroundRunning: typeof timeWash !== 'undefined' && timeWash !== null,\n      edgeDarkening: edgeDarkeningEnabled,\n      fadePainting: fadeEnabled,\n      pauseDrying: typeof dryingPaused !== 'undefined' ? dryingPaused : false,\n      gouacheMode: _gouacheMode,\n    };\n  },\n\n  // v0.52 \u2014 Preset save/load. Captures every user-controllable setting\n  // (brush, paper, evaporation, modes, fade, animation, etc.) into a\n  // plain serializable object that can be JSON-stringified, saved to\n  // localStorage, shared between sessions, posted to a gist, etc.\n  //\n  // Excludes: the actual painted artwork (d[]/g[] arrays \u2014 that's\n  // *content*, not settings), cursor state, system-dependent toggles\n  // (WebGL, mobile mode), and debug instrumentation (perf overlay).\n  //\n  // The shape is documented by `version: 1`. Future preset formats\n  // can bump the version and applyPreset can migrate forward; v1\n  // presets will continue to apply correctly to forward-compatible\n  // lib versions (unknown fields are silently ignored).\n  getPreset() {\n    // Derive evaporation multiplier from the stored rate.\n    // setEvaporationMult does evapRate = 0.9988^mult, so:\n    //   mult = log(rate) / log(0.9988)\n    // Round to avoid tiny float artifacts in serialization.\n    let evapMult = Math.log(evaporationRate) / Math.log(0.9988);\n    if (!isFinite(evapMult)) evapMult = 1;\n    evapMult = Math.round(evapMult * 100) / 100;\n    return {\n      version: 1,\n      // Brush\n      brushSize:     brushSize,\n      pigment:       currentPigment,\n      pressure:      _brushPressure,\n      flow:          _brushFlow,\n      paintLoad:     paintLoadMult,\n      waterLoad:     waterLoadMult,\n      // Paper / sim\n      paperColor:    { r: PAPER_R_BASE, g: PAPER_G_BASE, b: PAPER_B_BASE },\n      evaporation:   evapMult,\n      gouacheMode:   _gouacheMode,\n      edgeDarkening: edgeDarkeningEnabled,\n      pauseDrying:   dryingPaused,\n      transparent:   transparentBg,\n      continuousFlow: _continuousFlow,\n      // Fade\n      fadePainting:   fadeEnabled,\n      fadeHalfLifeMs: fadeHalfLifeMs,\n      // Animation / visualization\n      animationMode:     typeof animationMode !== 'undefined' ? animationMode : 'off',\n      visualizationMode: typeof visualizationMode !== 'undefined' ? visualizationMode : 'off',\n    };\n  },\n\n  // applyPreset(obj) \u2014 restore settings from a preset object. Each\n  // field is applied via the existing public API method, so all the\n  // side effects (events, re-LERPs, re-renders, swatch refreshes)\n  // fire naturally. Missing fields are skipped; unknown fields are\n  // ignored. Order matters in a few cases:\n  //   - paperColor before gouacheMode, so auto-LERP uses the new paper\n  //   - animation/visualization last, since they may inject state\n  //     that depends on other settings (paper color, pigment choice)\n  // After all fields are applied, dispatches 'presetapplied' on the\n  // host element so wiring can update sliders, swatches, toggles,\n  // etc. to match the new state.\n  applyPreset(p) {\n    if (!p || typeof p !== 'object') {\n      throw new Error('applyPreset: expected a preset object');\n    }\n    if (p.version !== undefined && p.version > 1) {\n      console.warn('applyPreset: preset version', p.version,\n        'is newer than this lib understands (v1). Applying best-effort.');\n    }\n    // Paper first \u2014 affects gouacheMode('auto') LERP downstream.\n    if (p.paperColor && typeof p.paperColor === 'object') {\n      api.paperColor(p.paperColor.r, p.paperColor.g, p.paperColor.b);\n    }\n    if (p.evaporation !== undefined)    api.evaporation(p.evaporation);\n    if (p.gouacheMode !== undefined)    api.gouacheMode(p.gouacheMode);\n    // Brush\n    if (p.brushSize !== undefined)      api.brushSize(p.brushSize);\n    if (p.pigment !== undefined)        api.pigment(p.pigment);\n    if (p.pressure !== undefined)       api.pressure(p.pressure);\n    if (p.flow !== undefined)           api.flow(p.flow);\n    if (p.paintLoad !== undefined)      api.paintLoad(p.paintLoad);\n    if (p.waterLoad !== undefined)      api.waterLoad(p.waterLoad);\n    // Sim toggles\n    if (p.edgeDarkening !== undefined)  api.edgeDarkening(p.edgeDarkening);\n    if (p.pauseDrying !== undefined)    api.pauseDrying(p.pauseDrying);\n    if (p.transparent !== undefined)    api.transparent(p.transparent);\n    if (p.continuousFlow !== undefined) api.continuousFlow(p.continuousFlow);\n    // Fade\n    if (p.fadeHalfLifeMs !== undefined) api.fadeHalfLife(p.fadeHalfLifeMs);\n    if (p.fadePainting !== undefined)   api.fadePainting(p.fadePainting);\n    // Animation / visualization last\n    if (p.animationMode !== undefined)\n      api.setAnimation(p.animationMode === 'off' ? null : p.animationMode);\n    if (p.visualizationMode !== undefined)\n      api.setVisualization(p.visualizationMode === 'off' ? null : p.visualizationMode);\n    targetEl.dispatchEvent(new CustomEvent('presetapplied', { detail: { preset: p } }));\n    return api;\n  },",
      "old_str": "  // State snapshot\n  state() {\n    return {\n      gridWidth: GW, gridHeight: GH,\n      displayWidth: DISPLAY_W, displayHeight: DISPLAY_H,\n      brushSize: brushSize,\n      pigment: currentPigment,\n      canvasScale: _canvasScale,\n      animationMode: typeof animationMode !== 'undefined' ? animationMode : 'off',\n      visualizationMode: typeof visualizationMode !== 'undefined' ? visualizationMode : 'off',\n      backgroundRunning: typeof timeWash !== 'undefined' && timeWash !== null,\n      edgeDarkening: edgeDarkeningEnabled,\n      fadePainting: fadeEnabled,\n      pauseDrying: typeof dryingPaused !== 'undefined' ? dryingPaused : false,\n      gouacheMode: _gouacheMode,\n    };\n  },",

OFF (default) \u2014 transparent watercolor pigments (Quinacridone Rose, Hansa Yellow, Cerulean Blue). Looks great on white/cream paper, nearly invisible on black. ON \u2014 opaque gouache pigments with high light-scattering (S) values, vibrant on any paper. AUTO (v0.51+) \u2014 LERPs between the two sets based on the perceptual luminance of the current paper color, so dragging the paper picker from cream to charcoal smoothly morphs the pigments from transparent watercolor to fully opaque gouache. The physically natural choice: light paper barely needs scattering pigments (transparent works); dark paper requires scattering (opaque works); papers in between get a proportional mix. Imagine a cup of water spilled violently across the page, or a pressure washer hitting the surface: a single point of impact with enough water, velocity, and pressure to push pigment anywhere on the canvas. Three modes selected by modifier key. Click alone \u2192 'water' (a single splash that lifts deposited pigment back into suspension and diffuses it outward, like a rinse). Shift-click \u2192 'pigment' (opaque wash in the currently-selected pigment color, like spilling colored paint). Alt-click \u2192 'paper' (clears the area back to canvas color, like an eraser). Where Big Splash distributes chaos across 4\\u20138 epicenters, Deluge concentrates it into ONE giant event \\u2014 flooded paper, strong radial flow at high velocity (~2.3\\u00d7 Big Splash), and a hard pressure spike that drives flow well beyond the splash ring. Imagine a cup of water spilled violently across the page, or a pressure washer hitting the surface: a single point of impact with enough water, velocity, and pressure to push pigment anywhere on the canvas. Lifts 95% of deposited pigment.\\\">Deluge<span class=\\\"info-marker\\\" aria-hidden=\\\"true\\\">\\u24d8</span></button>\\n      <button class=\\\"btn\\\"       id=\\\"obliterate-btn\\\"\\n              data-tip=\\\"Obliterate: destroys the current drawing via a wet wash centered on the canvas. Three modes selected by modifier key. Click alone \\u2192 'water' (a single splash that lifts deposited pigment back into suspension and diffuses it outward, like a rinse). Shift-click \\u2192 'pigment' (opaque wash in the currently-selected pigment color, like spilling colored paint). Alt-click \\u2192 'paper' (clears the area back to canvas color, like an eraser). All complete in 500ms. Equivalent to Watercolor.obliterate({ mode, durationMs: 500 }).\\\">Obliterate<span class=\\\"info-marker\\\" aria-hidden=\\\"true\\\">\\u24d8</span></button>\\n      <button class=\\\"btn\\\"       id=\\\"dry-btn\\\"\\n              data-tip=\\\"Instantly evaporates all water. Any suspended pigment locks in place as deposited.\\\">Dry<span class=\\\"info-marker\\\" aria-hidden=\\\"true\\\">\\u24d8</span></button>\\n      <button class=\\\"btn\\\"       id=\\\"remove-mask-btn\\\"\\n              data-tip=\\\"Removes all masking fluid in one go \\u2014 like rubbing it off a dry painting. Reveals whatever was frozen underneath.\\\">Remove mask<span class=\\\"info-marker\\\" aria-hidden=\\\"true\\\">\\u24d8</span></button>\\n      <button class=\\\"btn\\\"       id=\\\"export-btn\\\"\\n              data-tip=\\\"Saves the current canvas as a PNG. Composites the paper color underneath unless Transparent is on.\\\">Export PNG<span class=\\\"info-marker\\\" aria-hidden=\\\"true\\\">\\u24d8</span></button>\\n      <button class=\\\"btn\\\"       id=\\\"reset\\\"\\n              data-tip=\\\"Clears all pigment and returns the canvas to fresh wet paper.\\\">Reset<span class=\\\"info-marker\\\" aria-hidden=\\\"true\\\">\\u24d8</span></button>\\n\", \"filename\": \"/home/claude/watercolor.html\"}"

{};\n    const obj = JSON.parse(raw);\n    return obj && typeof obj === 'object' ? obj : {};\n  } catch (e) {\n    console.warn('preset load: corrupt localStorage, ignoring', e);\n    return {};\n  }\n}\nfunction saveUserPresets(map) {\n  try {\n    localStorage.setItem(PRESETS_LS_KEY, JSON.stringify(map));\n  } catch (e) {\n    console.warn('preset save: localStorage write failed', e);\n  }\n}\n\nconst presetsSelect = document.getElementById('presets-select');\nconst presetsSave   = document.getElementById('presets-save');\nconst presetsDelete = any exist)\n  const userNames = Object.keys(user);\n  if (userNames.length) {\n    const ogUser = document.createElement('optgroup');\n    ogUser.label = 'Saved';\n    for (const name of userNames) user[name];\n  if (!preset) return;\n  try {\n    wc.applyPreset(preset);\n  } catch (e) {\n    console.error('preset apply failed:', e);\n    alert('Failed to apply preset: ' + (e.message || e));\n  }\n}\n\nif (presetsSelect) {\n  populatePresetsDropdown();\n  presetsSelect.addEventListener('change', () => {\n    applyPresetByName(presetsSelect.value);\n  });\n}\n\nif (presetsSave) {\n '');\n    if (name == null) return;\n    const trimmed = name.trim();\n    if (!trimmed) return;\n    if (BUILTIN_NAMES.has(trimmed)) {\n      alert('\"' + trimmed + '\" is a built-in preset name. Pick a different name.');\n      return;\n    }\n    const user = loadUserPresets();\n    user[trimmed] = wc.getPreset();\n    saveUserPresets(user);\n    populatePresetsDropdown(trimmed);\n  });\n}\n\nif (presetsDelete) {\n  presetsDelete.addEventListener('click', () => {\n    const name = presetsSelect && presetsSelect.value;\n    if (!name) {\n      alert('Select a preset first.');\n      return;\n    }\n    if (BUILTIN_NAMES.has(name)) {\n      alert('Built-in presets can\\'t be deleted.');\n      return;\n    }\n    const user = loadUserPresets();\n    if (!user[name]) {\n      alert('Preset not found.');\n      return;\n    }\n    if (!confirm('Delete preset \"' + name + '\"? This cannot be undone.')) return;\n    delete user[name];\n    saveUserPresets(user);\n    populatePresetsDropdown();\n  });\n}\n\n// v0.52 \u2014 listen for presetapplied events to refresh other UI bits\n// (sliders, swatches, toggles) so they reflect the loaded state.\nhost.addEventListener('presetapplied', () => {\n  // Re-sync everything that mirrors lib state into the DOM.
